column3:
Nathalie‡"Lien vers les données
Jeux de données SIG et débits mensuels par bassins versants d'Afrique de l'Ouest et Centrale. Les fichiers fournis sont des fichiers d'entrée des modèles (couches vecteur SIG au format shapefile) des bassins versants constituées à partir des données cartographiques des 356 bassins référencés sous la base SIEREM. http://www.hydrosciences.fr/sierem/produits/produits/livret.pdf La principale station de mesure hydrométrique du bassin versant de l'El Beid est :
  • Rivière El Beid à Fotokol Gambarou code 1058009503
Tous les fichiers de données sont au format ArcGIS (shapefiles) :
  • les contours des bassins versants sont représentés par des polygones (fichier : SIEREM_Elbeid_Cameroon_Rivers_Basin)
  • le réseau hydrographique du bassin versant est représenté linéairement (fichier : Sierem_ElBeid_Cameroon_Rivers )

Le fichier de métadonnées au format xml créé avec le logiciel ArcCatalog répond à la norme ISO 19115 relative aux métadonnées géographiques.
Pour chaque station une chronique de débits mensuels (en l/s) issus du système d'informations SIEREM complète le jeu de données (format texte).
  • En décembre 2025 dans le but de de fournir

column3:
Frédéric‡"Lien vers les données

Contents of the ASMI

The Annales du Service Météorologique de l'Indochine is the third main journal of the French Indochinese Meteorological Service after the Bulletin Mensuel des Observations and the Bulletin Pluviométrique. The complete collection constitutes a set of 13 volumes published from 1930 to 1942 that is fully available in the National archives of Vietnam
column4: Centre n°1
column5:
Hanoi and also in Météo France Trappes centre.

The first year of edition (1930) published the data of 1928
column6: and the last year of edition (1942) the ones of 1940. The first volume of 1930 also published the monthly averages of the period 1906-1928. The data of this period have of very high interest
column7:
since they are the most reliable data we have for this early period. Hence climate historians can consider this period 1906-1928 in the ASMI as a period of reference with reliable observations to observe the climate change in this part of the world in one century.

The Annals are divided into 4 main parts:

  1. Climatology (that consists
column8: each year
column9: in a description of the network stations
column10:
and an overview of the year's weather in each country of the French Indochinese Union)
  • Meteorological data from the Phu Liên central Observatory
  • Meteorological data from the network stations
  • Rainfall data

    • The first part describes the station networks and equipment
column11: giving a general description of the year's weather in the various countries of the Indochinese Union
column12: reviewing the main data (temperature
column13: rainfall
column14: typhoons)
column15: and describing the influence of meteorological and climatic factors on indigenous agriculture and European agriculture in each country of the Union. The text contains numerous tables of important data
column16: including the approximate geographical coordinates of all the weather
column17: climate and rainfall stations in the network. The text also contains numerous maps of the network of different types of stations
column18:
as well as hypsometric maps.

The second part
column19: although entitled ‘daily observations'
column20: actually gives the monthly averages of these daily data. The second part contains the following tables (the semicolon separations indicate the columns of data
column21:
the rows being the 12 months of the year)

Contents of the dataset

The present dataset contains the full 4 parts of the 13 volumes. Each year is divided into 4 files (pdf) corresponding to each part
column22:
so as to make it easier for users to access the part of interest to them.

60 ans de pluies mensuelles en Afrique - Période 1940-1999 HydroSciences Montpellier a hérité du laboratoire d'hydrologie de l'IRD
column4: d'une très importante base de données hydroclimatiques sur l'Afrique de l'Ouest et Centrale
column5: nommée SIEREM. Différents programmes de recherche ont permis aux hydrologues d'HSM d'enrichir cette base sur la période post 1980 et sur la totalité du continent africain. Un long travail de critique a permis de constituer une base de données de référence des pluies d'Afrique au pas de temps mensuel. En raison de données trop rares et trop hétérogènes sur le début du XXème siècle
column6: les grilles ont été calculées de 1940 à 1999 (720 mois).<li> Nous proposons en téléchargement les grilles (un fichier par décennie
column7: 120 grilles par fichier) calculées à partir des données observées aux stations. La méthode est l'Inverse Distance Pondérée
column8: le pas de temps est mensuel et l'échelle spatiale est le demi-degré carré.<li> Un fichier .pdf à l'intérieur de chaque fichier .zip détaille le mode opératoire de la confection des grilles et la description du fichier. La carte des pluies est disponible au Service Editions IRD."‡IRD; Afrique; Scientific information systems; Precipitation; Grilles de pluie mensuelle; Monthly rainfall grids; Hydrological modelling; Africa; Cartography; Geography

The system of informal waste collectors and aggregators in Da Nang city (Vietnam) was surveyed in the framework of the COMPOSE project (2019-2021).

The social survey based on questionnaire was carried out in the entire area of Da Nang city to identify the location of the aggregator warehouse participating to the informal waste collection and recycling in the city. Then
column6: the collectors and aggregators were interviewed to collect information on the space
column7: architectural characteristic of the warehouse
column8: the neighborhood
column9: the hours of operation
column10: the type of materials collected and on the owners of the warehouse (hometown
column11:
the reasons for choosing the job). The surveys were conducted in August 2019 and June 2020.

Data anonymization was performed by removing identifiers
column12: generalization (grouping precise values into less precise/larger categories) and finally by aggregating individual records

column2: the data are available upon request. Please contact Valérie Poncet (valerie.poncet@ird.fr) and inform us of intended use for the data.‡Genotyping data for field samples from Haitian coffee agroforestry systems and international collection accessions‡Millet
column3: Claude Patrick‡Millet
column4:
Claude Patrick‡"Lien vers les données

Though facing significant challenges
column5: Coffee (Coffea arabica) grown in Haitian agroforestry systems are important contributors to rural livelihoods and provide several ecosystem services. There has been little work done on their genetic diversity and the variety mixtures used
column6: and there is a need to characterize Haitian coffee diversity to help inform revitalization of this sector. We sampled 28  agroforestry systems in historically important coffee growing regions of Northern and Southern Haiti. We performed Hi-Plex targeted multiplex amplicon sequencing and KASP-genotyping of SNP markers on our samples
column7:
as well as several Ethiopian and commercial accessions from international collections.
Here we provide the data acquired from this work
column8:
in four files:

Millet23_coffee_1_reference_info: This file contains information about the various Coffea reference samples from international collections used in the study
column9: including the accession information
column10: species
column11: and origin (where applicable). Some reference data are available for the KASP genotyping assay method
column12:
but not for HiPlex targeted sequencing. These are noted.

Millet23_coffee_2_field_sample_info: This file contains information about the Coffee field samples collected in Haiti
column13: including the geographic location
column14:
date of collection and local identification.

The Millennium Coral Reef Mapping Project provides thematic maps of coral reefs worldwide at geomorphological scale. Maps were created by photo-interpretation of Landsat 7 and Landsat 8 satellite images.

Maps are provided as standard Shapefiles usable in GIS software. The geomorphological classification scheme is hierarchical and includes 5 levels. The GIS products include for each polygon a number of attributes. The 5 level geomorphological attributes are provided (numerical codes or text). The Level 1 corresponds to the differentiation between oceanic and continental reefs. Then from Levels 2 to 5
column4: the higher the level
column5: the more detailed the thematic classification is. Other binary attributes specify for each polygon if it belongs to terrestrial area (LAND attribute)
column6: and sedimentary or hard-bottom reef areas (REEF attribute).  Examples and more details on the attributes are provided in the references cited. The products distributed here were created by IRD
column7:
in their last version.

Shapefiles for 52 atolls of the Indian Ocean and Red Sea as mapped by the Global coral reef mapping project at geomorphological scale using LANDSAT satellite data (L7 and L8). The data set provides one zip file per country or region of interest. Global coral reef mapping project at geomorphological scale using LANDSAT satellite data (L7 and L8).

column3: Claire‡Gondard
column4:
Claire‡"Lien vers les données

L'étude MAPNET ""Mapping social networks and community strategies in Southern Madagascar"" est une expertise collective conduite en 2019 pour la Banque mondiale.

A quelle protection sociale les habitants du Sud de Madagascar ont-ils effectivement accès ? Alors que la politique nationale de protection sociale est en pleine reconfiguration à Madagascar
column5: le taux de couverture de la population reste très faible et les dispositifs diffèrent largement d'une région à l'autre. Afin d'étudier les mécanismes de protection sociale effectivement accessibles localement
column6: nous proposons la notion de système local de protection sociale
column7: ou SLPS. Composante du système socio-écologique local
column8: le SLPS produit et régule les pratiques de protection sociales accessibles au niveau local. Il est le produit d'interactions sociales multiples
column9: régulées par les rapports sociaux encadrant les règles de réciprocité et de pouvoir. A partir de données empiriques originales
column10: cette étude vise à caractériser les formes prises par le système local de protection sociale en pays Tandroy
column11: région particulièrement vulnérable du Sud de Madagascar
column12: et à étudier les inégalités d'accès à ce système
column13: en fonction de caractéristiques socio-économiques-clé. La compréhension des dynamiques locales à différentes échelles (fokontany
column14: ménage
column15: personne) oblige à privilégier une approche mixte de production de donnée
column16: articulant des éléments de diagnostic systémique
column19: et d'entretiens collectifs auprès des organisations locales. L'enquête s'est déroulée dans 6 sites : Beheloka et Maniry (zone Mahafaly)
column20: Ambahitra et Ambazoa (zone Androy)
column21:
Mahatalaky et Ifotaka (zone Anosy)

Nous présentons ici les outils méthodologiques
column22: les données d'enquête et la documentation des 3 enquêtes de l'étude MAPNET : l'enquête systémique
column23:
l'enquête organisations locales (OL) et l'enquête ménages-individus-réseaux (enquête MIR).

The plant-parasitic nematode Meloidogyne graminicola causes considerable damages to rice (Oryza sativa) culture. Resistance to M. graminicola in the related species Oryza glaberrima reduces root penetration by juveniles and stops further nematode development. M. graminicola genes expressed during O. sativa (Nipponbare) and O. glaberrima (TOG5681) infection were characterized at different time after infection.

M. graminicola RNA-Seq data were retrieved from 14 (7 × 2 replicates) cDNA libraries obtained from pre-parasitic M. graminicola stage-two juveniles (J2s)
column5: and from O. sativa cv Nipponbare (susceptible) or O. glaberrima TOG5681 (resistant) plants challenged with M. graminicola. Root tips and galls
column6: when formed
column7: were collected at 2
column9: or 8 dpi. Time points 2
column11: and 8 dpi were chosen to focus on the early stages of the interaction
column12:
when gene expression differences may determine the nature of interaction between both partners.

We generated a de novo M. graminicola transcriptome composed of 44
column13: 137 sequences. We compared the M. graminicola transcriptomes before and during infection to identify M. graminicola genes differentially expressed at 2
column14:
4 and 8 days after infection of susceptible (Nipponbare) or resistant (TOG5681) rice.

Fontaine MC. 2023. Harbor porpoise losing its edges: genetic time series suggests a rapid population decline in Iberian waters over the last 30 years. Accepted in the journal Ecology and Evolution (Wiley) (biorXiv preprint doi: https://doi.org/10.1101/2021.08.19.456945)

Content summary
- Input data and scripts required to replicate the demographic inference by Approximate Bayesian Computation (ABC).
- Mitochondrial DNA sequence alignments generated in the present study.
- Input data and scripts used to compute the Hudson et al 1992 estimator of FST as well as the Excoffier et al 1992 estimator of PhiST.
- Input data and script used to compute haplotype diversity in each population.
- Input data and settings used to build the phylogenetic tree.
- Input data and script to replicate the rarefaction analysis for the estimators of mtDNA genetic diversity.

Research abstract
Impact of climate changes on species is expected to be especially visible at the extremities of the species distribution
column12: where they meet sub-optimal conditions. In Mauritania and Iberia
column13: two genetically isolated populations of harbor porpoises form a distinct ecotype and are presumably locally adapted to the upwelling waters. By analyzing the evolution of mitochondrial genetic variation in the Iberian population between two temporal cohorts (1990-2002 vs. 2012-2015)
column14: we report a dramatic decrease in genetic diversity. Phylogenetic analyses including neighboring populations identified two porpoises in southern Iberia carrying a divergent haplotype close to the Mauritanian population
column15: yet forming a distinctive lineage. This suggests that Iberian porpoises may not be as isolated as previously thought with immigration from Mauritania or an unknown population in between
column16: but none from the northern ecotype. The rapid decline in the Iberian mitochondrial diversity may be driven by either genetic drift
column17: or by a dramatic decline in census population size possibly resulting from environmental stochasticity
column19: or acute fishery bycatches. These results illustrate the value of genetics time series to inform demographic trends and emphasize the urgent need for conservation measures to ensure the viability of this small harbor porpoise population in Iberia."‡IRD; cetacean conservation genetics; genetic time series; genetic diversity; approximate Bayesian computation; population genetic modelling; Aquatic ecosystems; Continental waters and oceans: biology; INRAE‡‡"ABC.tar.gz; Alignments.tar.gz; BenChehida_et_al2023_Iberian_porpoises_mtDNA_README.txt; FST.tar.gz; Haplotype_diversity.tar.gz; Phylogenetic_tree.tar.gz; Rarefaction.tar.gz"‡‡‡English‡-20.214843750000004‡43.94531250000001‡72.01972876525517‡14.093957177836236‡

Collected in Senegal from 2015 to 2020
column4: the dataset comprises 169 patients (subjects)
column5: children and adolescents living with HIV since childhood
column6: and 250 demographic
column7:
psychosocial and virological descriptive variables.
The data are longitudinal in nature
column8: since for each patient the value of the viral load before HIV disclosure status
column9: during the first year post-disclosure and during the second year post-disclosure is recorded. In other words
column10: three successive measures. The 3 measures are presented in columns (not rows).  These data were collected as part of a research project from 1 January 2015 to 31 December 2016
column11: and then in routine practice until 31 December 2020. Since 2021
column12: the data has been subject to in-depth cleaning and a search for missing data (viral load results). They were analysed retrospectively in 2022.  An article was then written
column13:
as summarized below.

Research abstract
HIV-status disclosure to children and adolescents living with HIV is undoubtedly one of the greatest challenges facing health care providers (HCP) in sub-Saharan Africa. The absence of standardised protocols and appropriate training for HIV disclosure and fears about its negative effects on adherence to antiretroviral treatment (ART) and follow-up have been identified as some of the barriers limiting disclosure to children and adolescents. We developed a standardised HIV-disclosure intervention involving HCP
column14: caretakers and children at the national pediatric clinic in Dakar
column15:
Senegal. This retrospective analysis describes the standardised intervention and the impact of improved disclosure of HIV status to children and adolescents on retention in care and virological suppression.

HCP were trained to use WHO-recommended practices for HIV disclosure. The intervention was structured around group preparation of caretakers and children during a pre-disclosure period
column19: and then post-disclosure follow-up to 24 months. For inclusion in the intervention
column20: children had to be at least seven years old
column21: with no mental disorders and with a reliable caretaker. Change in virological status was assessed only in children on ART
column22:
with at least one viral load in the pre- and post-disclosure periods using the MacNemar test for matched samples. Factors associated with post-disclosure virological failure (viral load > 1000 copies/mL) were investigated in the participants' characteristics using a logistic regression model.

From 2015 to 2020
column23: 169 children and adolescents were enrolled in the intervention (sex ratio: 1.2)
column24: of which 77% were on ART. HIV status was disclosed to all enrollees at a median age of 12.8 years (IQR = 11.9 – 14.1). HCP carried out all disclosures. Full disclosure (including the mode of transmission to the child) was possible for 85% of participants. At 24 months post-disclosure
column25: the retention rate was 98%. All enrollees not on ART initiated therapy within one month (0 – 3) post disclosure. Of the 115 children on ART at disclosure
column26: and with pre- and post-disclosure viral load measurements available
column27: the proportion demonstrating virological success (78%) remained unchanged (MacNemar's paired test
column28: 24 months p= 0.99). Virological failure at disclosure (aOR= 2.8
column29: 95% CI: 1.1 – 7.5) and being on second-line ART at disclosure (2.8
column30:
1.2 – 6.7) were associated with virological failure at 24 months.

This study shows that it is possible to involve children who have achieved virological success
column32:
in a comprehensive HIV-disclosure intervention without a major impact on their medium-term adherence to ART and retention in care. This innovative intervention for preparing and supporting children and adolescents and their caretakers for HIV disclosure provides a valuable scientific and experiential model for implementing and evaluating good disclosure practices.

column3:
Thomas‡"Lien vers les données

The ability of species and populations to adapt to their environment faces increasing challenges as climate change accelerates. Recent methods based on genomic offset (GO) statistics aim to quantify the risk of non-adaptation of populations to future climates. While several studies have evaluated the ability of different offset statistics to predict population (mal)adaptation
column4:
the impact of the chosen climate data —which could vary in relevance and quality— remains unexplored.

To explore this question
column5: we analyzed the case of 157 pearl millet landraces cultivated in West Africa
column6: for which fitness proxies were measured during field trials conducted over two years. We calculated geometric and gradient forest genomic offset statistics using three different historical climate datasets: bioclimatic variables from WorldClim (v.2.1)
column7: from CHELSA (v.2.1)
column8:
and a dataset specifically designed for this study derived from the EWEMBI (v.1) climate database.

We provide here the climate data computed from the EWEMBI v.1 dataset
column9:
along with the code used to extract the climate crop-specific metrics.

column5: fats and essential omega-3
column6: and considered also the omega-6/omega-3 ratio and mercury content. The benefit-risk assessment used a hazard quotient for three people categories: young children
column7:
children and adults. The influence of fish length on the nutritional composition of muscle was also assessed.

The dataset provides total mercury
column8: EPA+DHA and protein concentrations for 175 fish individuals of 20 species taken as bycatch in tropical tuna fisheries of the western Indian Ocean
column9:
and landed in Seychelles in 2018 (18 fishing dates/geographical positions).

column11: and 24 columns presenting samples' ID
column12: taxonomic rank

Data were collected as part of the ANR project ENEMI
column5: which aimed at investigating the role of parasitism and immunology in the invasion success of the house mouse Mus musculus domesticus in Senegal. They were issued from 6 field campaigns conducted between November 2011 and July 2014 in villages and towns of the northern part of the country: in the anciently invaded area along the coast from Saint-Louis to Mbour
column6: and along two invasions routes
column7: one along the northern road in the Senegal River valley
column8:
and the second along the main road across the Ferlo region.

Using standard trapping procedures
column9: commensal small mammals were captured
column10: then euthanized
column11: sexed and measured
column12: identified using morphological or molecular tools
column13: and finally autopsied in order to take a number of samples intended for the detection of parasites and pathogens
column14: the characterization of immune profiles
column15:
as well as the study of the population genetics of some species. Commensal habitat characteristics were recorded at each trap position.

The dataset reports the results of 9660 trapnights
column16: leading to the capture of 1987 small mammals (mainly native and exotic rodents - 7 species
column17: and a few species of shrews and hedgehogs) and the collection of associated biological
column18: geographical and environmental data. It should provide opportunities for studies in population and community ecology at various spatial scales. It perfectly supplement the dataset already deposited by Granjon & Fossati entitled ""<a href=""https://doi.org/10.23708/PQTQDA"">Commensal small mammal trapping data in Southern Senegal
column20: and collected at the same period using exactly the same protocol during another research program. Combined with data obtained through the treatment of associated biological samples (concerning immune profiles and parasite/pathogen detection
column21: see references)
column22:
both datasets may contribute to characterise zoonotic risks related to small mammals in Senegal.

column3:
Frédéric‡"Lien vers les données

Content of the ABCM

The Annales du Bureau Central Météorologique first appeared in 1880 under the direction of Éleuthère Mascart. The ABCM succeeded the Bulletin météorologique de l'Association scientifique de France
column4: published from 1871 until 1876 by M. Bellegrand. The latter focused mainly on the publication of rainfall data for river basins
column5: with the main aim of forecasting floods. The ABCM gradually extended the scope of the data published to include temperature
column6: pressure
column7: humidity
column8:
etc.

The first issue in 1882 published data for 1878
column9: then for 1877. The last volume
column10: published in 1926
column11:
published data for 1920.

From 1882 to 1885
column12:
the Annales were divided into 4 volumes (32 volumes in all)
  1. Studies of thunderstorms in France ;
  2. Bulletin des observations françaises and revue climatologique
  3. Rainfall in France
  4. General meteorology
From 1886 to 1914
column13:
the Annales were divided into 3 volumes (87 volumes in total)
  1. Memoirs
  2. Observations
  3. Rainfall in France
The volumes 2 of observations were themselves divided into three sections:
  • A. French observations (pages numbered A3 to A...);
  • B. Algerian observations (numbered B3 to B...);
  • C. Observations done in French colonies and abroad (numbered C3 to C...)

It was in this last section C that the first meteorological data from Indo-China were published from 1890 onwards.

From 1915 to 1920
column14:
only 4 volumes were published (2 volumes for the years 1915-1917 and 2 volumes for the years 1918-1920). These 4 volumes contained no data for Indochina.

In 1924
column15: data for the years 1915
column16: 1916 and 1917 were published. The introduction to this volume states that for a long time it had been difficult for the Central Office to compile the 1915 data. It also states that observations made in the colonies were no longer published in the ABCM from that date onwards. For observations made in Algeria
column18: it is specified that these data are published by the meteorological services of these countries
column19: for the other colonies and in particular for Indochina
column20:
the introduction to this volume does not specify the periodical in which they can be found.

Contents of the dataset

This dataset contains all the Indochinese station data published between 1890 and 1914 in part 3 of volume 2 of the Annales (of which we have made an exhaustive inventory).

Meteorological data coverage

As part of the MorLo project
column5: this cross-sectional study assessed blood samples from 38 people living in an area of Cameroon where loiasis is endemic. We evaluated the impact of loiasis and its treatment with ivermectin on hematological
column6: hemostatic
column7: and biochemical blood parameters (complete blood counts
column8: erythrocyte sedimentation rate
column9: C-reactive protein
column10: prothrombin time

column14: ferritin
column15: D-dimers
column16:
prothrombin fragment F1+2 and plasminogen). Participants were divided into four balanced groups based on their Loa loa microfilarial densities.

At baseline
column17: eosinophils (p<0.001) and platelets (p=0.044) increased with microfilarial densities. At day 4 following ivermectin administration
column19: 095/µL (p<0.001) and median D-dimers increased from 582 ng/mL to 745 ng/mL (p=0.024) with a median relative variation of +34.7%. C-reactive protein
column20: fibrinogen
column21:
and alpha-1-globulin also increased significantly after treatment.

Ivermectin treatment appeared to induce inflammation
column22:
coagulation activation and fibrinolysis.

column3: 2016-2022‡Rodrigues Do Amaral

This dataset is the result of a publication that explores calibration strategies and operational implications of 1D hydrodynamic modeling using the Mage code in the Saigon-Dongnai system in Vietnam. Three calibration approaches for the Mage model are evaluated
column6: and coupling with the Modified Manning-Strickler (MMS) equation is explored for discharge estimation enhancement. Calibration efforts reveal distinct trends in friction coefficients along the river
column7: with the introduction of indirectly measured discharge data significantly improving model performance
column8:
particularly for the Saigon River branch.

This model output has been validated against independent measurements and demonstrates promising results
column9:
with the MMS coupling providing improved discharge estimates in the Saigon branch.

The long time-series of water level and discharge (January 2016 to December 2022) provided in this dataset have practical implications for water resource management and decision-making in estuarine systems and is provided here in open-access for operational use.

double-blind clinical trial‡Jérémy T Campillo‡Cédric B. Chesnais‡"Lien vers les données
Background. Individuals with high Loa loa microfilarial densities (MFD) risk serious adverse events (SAEs) following ivermectin treatment. A single dose of levamisole (LEV) induces a temporary
column4: progressive MFD decrease. We assessed the safety and efficacy of 3- and 5-day LEV (LEV-3 and LEV-5) regimens for loiasis treatment.  Methods. A double-blind
column5: randomized
column6: placebo-controlled clinical trial in the Republic of the Congo assigned participants (1:1:1) to receive PLA
column7: LEV-3 or LEV-5. Sample size calculation was based on data from a previous clinical trial evaluating LEV efficacy on L. loa MFD. Safety (occurrence of SAEs
column8: frequency of AEs) was analysed using Fischer's exact test and logistic regression. Efficacy (MFD reduction and proportion of individuals achieving 40% reduction) was assessed through non parametric tests
column9: adjusted for multiple comparisons. A mixed-effects linear model evaluated MFD changes
column10: with post hoc contrasts.  Results. No SAEs occurred

This dataset contains 285 Vis-NIR and NIR spectra
column4: measured over soil samples collected over two sites
column5:
Châteaudun and Melle.

Soil samples

285 soil samples were collected in March 2014 over two agroforestry fields
column6: located in Châteaudun (Eure-et-Loir
column7: France) and Melle (Deux-Sèvres
column8:
France).

Spectral acquisitions

The VisNIR reflectance spectra were measured directly on the field
column9: using a portable spectrophotometer LabSpec 2500 (ASD
column10: Boulder
column11: CO
column12: USA). The spectral resolution ranged from 350 to 2500 nm
column13: with a 1 nm interval. Each reflectance resulted from the averaging of 32 co-added scans. On the field
column14: soil auger cores were sampled at three points for each sampling location. On each core
column15: one spectrum was acquired at 3 and 7 cm for 0-10 cm layer
column16:
13 and 17 cm for the 10-20 cm layer and 23 and 27 cm for the 20-30 cm layer. The six spectra acquired per layer were then averaged.
The NIR reflectance spectra were measured in the lab
column19: USA). The spectral resolution ranged from 1100 to 2498 nm
column20: with a 2 nm interval. Each spectrum was the result of 32 co-added scans and for each sample
column21:
two replications were performed. The two spectra were then averaged.
All spectra were then converted from reflectance (R) to absorbance (A) : A = - log(R). Every spectrum in these datasets is an absorbance spectrum.

SOC determination

SOC content was determined by dry combustion
column22: using an Elemantal Analyser (CHN Fisons/Carlo Erba NA 2000
column23: Milan
column24: Italy). SOC content is here expressed in g/kg.  SOC stock was computed as the product between the bulk density (calculated as the ratio of the mass of dry fine earth (<2mm) to the cylinder volume) and the SOC content. SOC stock is here expressed in kg/L. For further details on SOC determination
column25:
see Cambou et al. (2016).

Projects & Fundings

This dataset is part of the INCA project (In-field spectroscopy for Carbon Accounting)
column26: that belongs to the GESSOL program of the French Ministry of Ecology
column27: sustainable development and energy and was funded by ADEME (Agence de l'environnement et de la maîtrise de l'énergie). This dataset is also part of the ALAMOD project (2024-2027)
column28: financed by the PEPR FairCarbon. The rice yellow mottle virus (RYMV) constitutes a major threat to the actual development of rice cultivation in Africa. As climate change and agricultural intensification increase the risk of epidemics

there is an urgent need to understand the spatio-temporal dynamics of RYMV at local scale.

This dataset provides information on the pathogenicity of RYMV isolates collected in western Burkina Faso between 2015 and 2021. Two experiments were conducted in controlled greenhouse conditions (IRD Montpellier
column5: France
column7: to characterize the phenotype of viral isolates from the major viral genetic groups identified in western Burkina Faso
column8: on a set of rice cultivars.  <ul><li>The first experiment aimed to assess the symptom severity and intra-plant viral accumulation of four viral isolates
column9: each representing one identified genetic group
column10:
on three rice cultivars from western Burkina Faso and 2 reference cultivars. Three successive replications were performed.
  • The second experiment aimed to test the pathogenicity of 13 viral isolates (including those used in the first experiment)
column11:
from the genetic groups identified in western Burkina Faso on one susceptible rice cultivar. The second experiment was all performed at the same time.

For both experiments
column12: induced symptoms (estimated with a specific severity scale)
column13: as well as intra-plant viral accumulation (estimated though quantitative PCR)
column14:
were evaluated for each viral isolate.

This experimental work evidenced no differences between the three rice cultivars cultivated in Burkina Faso
column15:
and no statistical effect of RYMV genetic group on symptom expression and viral load. We found however that RYMV isolates differed from each other and that the three isolates resulting in strongest symptoms and highest viral loads are from the same lineage (S1bzn).

This dataset was acquired within the framework of several successive French-Brazilian projects with the objective of characterizing the spatio-temporal variations of water quality in the Curuai floodplain.
It includes data that were acquired between March 2013 and September 2017 during 9 field campaigns conducted at different hydrological periods. In each campaign we visited at least 25 fixed stations distributed in the different lakes that compose the floodplain. The positions of these stations are distributed along north-south transects in order to evaluate the potential variations according to the distance to the upland and the degree of connection with the river.
The campaigns were organized at contrasting hydrological periods
column6:
making it possible to cover a relatively broad spectrum of water levels (and thus of flood extent and stored water volume) in the floodplain.

chlorophyll-a and/or turbidity).

The Red River brings to the Gulf of Tonkin considerable quantities of fresh water (3700 m3/s) and suspended matter (45 Mt/year). This region
column14: whose coasts are very densely populated (approx. 30M inhabitants in the Red River delta)
column15: is subject to variability factors of different origins (atmospheric
column19: from extreme events (cyclones) to climate change (sea level rise is particularly sensitive in the delta region).  In estuaries
column20:
less dense freshwater flows over marine salt water with great variability in space and time.

As a continuation of this work
column21: new projects are currently starting on the analysis of river plumes in the Haiphong area (Cam Bach Dang and Van Uc rivers): <UL> <LI>Plume dispersion analysis based on monitoring of Lagrangian drifters (Alexei Sentchev
column23: ADCP) <LI>Concomitant measurements of suspended particles (turbidity
column25: grain size distribution) <LI>In-situ characterization of the spatial and temporal variability of suspended matter (in-situ measurement of grain size distribution
column27:
long-shore and cross-shore variability)

The Van Uc River is a typical distributary of the Red River
column28: with rice crops all along the banks that require water and construction of dikes for flooding protection
column29: and with constant river bed dredging for navigation purposes. The Van Uc estuary is located next to Haiphong city (third largest city of Vietnam). It varies in width from ~350 to ~450 m. The Van Uc River is ranked at the third position of the Red River system in terms of both water (14.5% of the total water discharge from the Red-Thai Binh system to the Gulf of Tonkin) and sediment discharge (14.4% of the total sediment flux of the Red-Thai Binh River to the Red River coastal area. Van Uc river is influenced by a strong seasonal river signal associated with the monsoon. Indeed
column30:
the wet summer season (June to September) accounts for 85-95% of the total annual rainfall in the region.

In-situ measurement campaigns were carried out to study estuarine processes. Field surveys have been performed in the Van uc estuary and in the close coastal plume at different stages of the wet season (June
column33:
always in spring tides.

Two sampling strategies have been employed. The first strategy consisted in longitudinal transects of around 15 to 20 km in the estuary performed at various tide levels. Stations were spaced around 2 km and efforts were made to bring closer together the stations where important turbidity was measured and when we discovered rapid change in salinity. The transects were made in around 3h and performed in both senses (from upstream to downstream and opposite). Transect stations extend from 13 km upstream to 9 km downstream the mouth.

The second measurement strategy consisted in a fixed station near the mouth of the river of 6h in June
column34:
in order to follow the evolution of the same part of the estuary over a specific tide period.

During these transects and fixed stations we measured continuously the current velocity Acoustic Doppler Current Profiler (ADCP). At each station
column37: density and turbidity were measured using a profiling CTD Compact. Another CTD
column39: was used to perform instant profiles and sharpen the transects strategy
column40:
i.e. adapting better the evolution in salinity.

In this study

we characterised for the first time through DNA metabarcoding the omnivorous diet of the African wolf.

column7: • trnl for plants (Taberlet et al. 2007; https://doi.org/10.1093/nar/gkl938). DNA extraction was carried out from a subsample of c. 200 mg of each faeces using the QIAamp DNA Stool kit (Qiagen
column8: Les Ulis
column9:
France) within the “Degraded DNA” platform of the Institut des Sciences de l'Evolution de Montpellier (ISEM).
In order to capture the broad dietary spectrum of the African wolf
column10: three genetic markers were used to detect vertebrates (mitochondrial 12S ribosomal RNA—12S)
column11:
arthropods (mitochondrial Cytochrome Oxidase subunit I—COI) and plants (chloroplastic trnl intron—trnl).
The amplicon libraries were built following a two-step amplification procedure adapted from the Illumina 16S Metagenomic Sequencing Library Preparation Guide (ref.15044223 rev. B).

column13: market gardening
column14: dense deciduous trees
column15: palm trees
column17:

The method used to generate the maps involved a supervised object-based image classification using aerial images acquired by an UAV
column18: a ground-truth dataset acquired by photo-interpretation
column19:
and a random forest classifier. The classification accuracy varies from 91% to 94% depending on the district.

These data were collected in the frame of Violaine Piton's PhD at LEGOS (Toulouse
column5: France)
column6: whose subject is to study the dynamics and sediment transport along the estuary-coastal area continuum. The evolution of the Red River delta in terms of siltation
column7: erosion and accretion of its estuaries and coastlines constitute a major concern
column8: as the delta is the most populated region of Vietnam
column9: and is the second-most important rice-producing region of the country. The Red River was ranked as the ninth river in the world in terms of sediment discharge but the construction of two dams across the river in the 1980s caused a reduction of sediment discharge from 145-160 Mt y-1 in the 1970s (Milliman and Meade
column10: 1983) to 40 Mt y-1 nowadays (Le et al.
column11: 2007; Vinh et al. 2014; Wei et al.
column12:
2019).

In order to explore the seasonal and tidal changes in flow and suspended matter dynamics in the tropical and macrotidal Van Uc river estuary (North Vietnam) and in order to better understand the sediment delivery to the ocean and the processes behind the estuarine siltation
column13:
four in-situ measurement campaigns were carried out in 2017.

The campaigns took place during the contrasting high flow and low flow seasons
column14: for each during neap and spring tides. This dataset corresponds to the wet season
column15: neap tide. Water and suspended matter fluxes
column17: turbidity and particle size distribution (PSD) were measured for 24h at three cross sections along the estuary. These three stations (ST1
column18: ST2 and ST3) are spaced by 10km. ST1 is located near to the river mouth while ST3 is the further upstream. Each station was sampled for 24 hours with cross-river transects measurements every two hours to cover a full diurnal tidal cycle
column19:
starting at ST1 and finishing at ST3.

Each series of measurements consisted of four different steps. Firstly
column20: cross river velocity profiles were measured will a hull-mounted
column21: downward-looking
column22: 1200 kHz Acoustic Doppler Current Profiler (ADCP RDI Workhorse in bottom tracking mode). Secondly
column23: vertical profiles of conductivity and temperature were measured for each transect
column24: in the deep channel at the middle of the river
column29: now released by JFE Advantech Co.
column32: as Rinko-Profiler) at a frequency of 0.1 Hz. Thirdly and parallel to CTD measurements
column33: depth profiles of floc size distribution and concentration were measured using an in-situ laser scattering and transmissometry instrument with a 90% path reduction module (LISST-100X
column39: in order to study the relationship between turbidity and suspended particulate matter concentration
column40:
water samples (~1 L) near subsurface (~1.5 m depth) were collected every four hours with a Niskin bottle. Turbidity of these samples were measured onboard using a Hach 2100Q Turbidimeter.

Pearl millet (Pennisetum glaucum (L) R. Br.) is a key cereal for food security in arid and semi-arid regions but its yield is increasingly threatened by water stress. These data were collected in the frame of Juliette Penicaud's PhD at LEGOS (Toulouse
column5: France)
column6: whose subject is the model-based study of dynamics and hydrosedimentary processes in the riverine estuary and in the plume of the Red River
column7: East Sea of Vietnam. The general objective of the thesis is to develop and deepen the expertise in terms of modelling and observation of the estuary and river plumes in the Haiphong area in order to reproduce their dynamics and to analyse the hydrosedimentary processes on the basis of numerical models
column8: benefiting for their calibration and validation from all archive or future data
column9: in situ or satellite. Supervision of the PhD: Sylvain Ouillon & Marine Herrmann (IRD

column11: with the strong involvement of Vu Duy Vinh (IMER
column12: VAST
column13:
Haiphong).

The Red River brings to the Gulf of Tonkin considerable quantities of fresh water (3700 m3/s) and suspended matter (45 Mt/year). This region
column14: whose coasts are very densely populated (approx. 30M inhabitants in the Red River delta)
column15: is subject to variability factors of different origins (atmospheric
column19: from extreme events (cyclones) to climate change (sea level rise is particularly sensitive in the delta region).  In estuaries
column20:
less dense freshwater flows over marine salt water with great variability in space and time.

As a continuation of this work
column21: new projects are currently starting on the analysis of river plumes in the Haiphong area (Cam Bach Dang and Van Uc rivers): <UL> <li>Plume dispersion analysis based on monitoring of Lagrangian drifters (Alexei Sentchev
column23: ADCP) <li>Concomitant measurements of suspended particles (turbidity
column25: grain size distribution) <li>In-situ characterization of the spatial and temporal variability of suspended matter (in-situ measurement of grain size distribution
column27:
long-shore and cross-shore variability)

The Van Uc River is a typical distributary of the Red River
column28: with rice crops all along the banks that require water and construction of dikes for flooding protection
column29: and with constant river bed dredging for navigation purposes. The Van Uc estuary is located next to Haiphong city (third largest city of Vietnam). It varies in width from ~350 to ~450 m. The Van Uc River is ranked at the third position of the Red River system in terms of both water (14.5% of the total water discharge from the Red-Thai Binh system to the Gulf of Tonkin) and sediment discharge (14.4% of the total sediment flux of the Red-Thai Binh River to the Red River coastal area. Van Uc river is influenced by a strong seasonal river signal associated with the monsoon. Indeed
column30:
the wet summer season (June to September) accounts for 85-95% of the total annual rainfall in the region.

In-situ measurement campaigns were carried out to study estuarine processes. Field surveys have been performed in the Van uc estuary and in the close coastal plume at different stages of the wet season (June
column33:
always in spring tides.

Two sampling strategies have been employed. The first strategy consisted in longitudinal transects of around 15 to 20 km in the estuary performed at various tide levels. Stations were spaced around 2 km and efforts were made to bring closer together the stations where important turbidity was measured and when we discovered rapid change in salinity. The transects were made in around 3h and performed in both senses (from upstream to downstream and opposite). Transect stations extend from 13 km upstream to 9 km downstream the mouth.

The second measurement strategy consisted in a fixed station near the mouth of the river of 24h in August
column34:
in order to follow the evolution of the same part of the estuary over the full tidal cycle.

During these transects and fixed stations we measured continuously the current velocity Acoustic Doppler Current Profiler (ADCP). At each station
column37: density and turbidity were measured using a profiling CTD Compact. Another CTD
column39: was used to perform instant profiles and sharpen the transects strategy
column40:
i.e. adapting better the evolution in salinity.

- Multiple alignments of DNA sequences of mitogenomes generated in the present study.
- Input data

- Codes and scripts are available via GitHub (https://github.com/jorgeamaya/automatic_genome_assembly and https://github.com/jorgeamaya/malaria_mitogenome).

Research abstract
Mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) has been a popular marker in phylogeography
column21: phylogeny
column23: but its complex evolution is increasingly recognized. Here
column24: we investigated mtDNA variation in Anopheles gambiae and Anopheles coluzzii
column25: in relation with other species in the Anopheles gambiae complex (AGC)
column26: by assembling the mitogenomes of 1219 mosquitoes across Africa. The mtDNA phylogeny of the AGC was consistent with previously reported highly reticulated evolutionary history
column27: revealing important discordances with the species tree. The three most widespread species (An. gambiae
column29: Anopheles arabiensis)
column31: could not be discriminated based on mitogenomes. Furthermore
column32: a monophyletic clustering of the three salt-water tolerant species (Anopheles merus
column34: Anopheles bwambae) in the AGC also suggested that introgression and possibly selection shaped mtDNA evolution. MtDNA variation in An. gambiae and An. coluzzii across Africa revealed significant partitioning among populations and species. A peculiar mtDNA lineage found predominantly in An. coluzzii and in the hybrid taxon of the African “far-west” exhibited divergence comparable to the inter-species divergence in the AGC
column35: with a geographic distribution matching closely An. coluzzii's geographic range. This phylogeographic relict of the An. coluzzii and An. gambiae split was associated with population and species structure
column36: but not with the rare Wolbachia occurrence. The lineage was significantly associated with SNPs in the nuclear genome
column37: particularly in genes associated with pathogen and insecticide resistance. These findings underline potential mito-nuclear coevolution history and the role played by mitochondria in shaping metabolic responses to pathogens and insecticides in Anopheles."‡IRD; Anopheles gambiae complex; mitogenome; mito-nuclear coevolution; phylogeography; insecticide resistance; OXSPHOS; Medical entomology

column3:
Shilpa‡"Lien vers les données
This dataset contains information on all coastal MHWs for 12 Pacific Island Countries and Territories (PICTs) in the SWTP.
MHW detection was done to understand the nature of these extreme temperature events along the coastlines of these PICTs and characterise them in terms of their properties including duration
column4:
maximum and cumulative intensities and onset and decline rates. Means and trends of these properties were explored as well. (Visit https://github.com/shilpa-s-lal/mhws for MHW analysis scripts.)
MHWs were detected using the Hobday et al.(2016) method with a 90th percentile threshold and using OSTIA temperature product for the period 1993 to 2023 with the 1993 to 2019 period used as a baseline.

The 12 PICTs are as follows:
  • New Caledonia labelled as ""newcal"" in the filenames.
  • Solomon Islands labelled as ""solo"" in the filenames.
  • Vanuatu
  • Fiji
  • Tonga
  • Wallis and Futuna labelled as ""wf"" in the filenames.
  • Samoa
  • American Samoa labelled as ""amsam"" in the filenames.
  • Niue
  • Tokelau
  • Tuvalu
  • Cook Islands labelled as ""cooks"" in the filenames.
Files with filename ""{country_name}_coastalpoints.csv""provides information on all points along the coastlines of the 12 PICTs where MHW detection was conducted.

Files with filename ""combined_mhw_output_{country_name}.csv""contains information on the properties of each MHW at each coastal point. Refer to Marine heatwaves package documents developed by Eric Oliver to understand the calculation of each MHW property (https://github.com/ecjoliver/marineHeatWaves).

Files with filename ""{country_name}.csv"" contains trends
column5:
means and standard deviation of MHW properties at each coastal point.

Geographic shapes are a list of rules implemented using the Shapes Constraint Language (SHACL).

Ce(tte) œuvre est mise à disposition selon les termes de la Licence Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International."‡Intervention reducing malaria parasite load in vector mosquitoes: no impact on Plasmodium falciparum extrinsic incubation period and the survival of Anopheles gambiae: analysis data and code‡Guissou
column3:
Edwige‡"Lien vers les données

In the fight against malaria
column4: transmission blocking interventions (TBIs) such as transmission blocking vaccines or drugs
column5: are promising approaches to complement conventional tools. They aim to prevent the infection of vectors and thereby reduce the subsequent exposure of a human population to infectious mosquitoes. The effectiveness of these approaches has been shown to depend on the initial intensity of infection in mosquitoes
column6: often measured as the mean number of oocysts resulting from an infectious blood meal in absence of intervention. In mosquitoes exposed to a high intensity of infection
column7: current TBI candidates are expected to be ineffective at completely blocking infection but will decrease parasite load and therefore
column8: potentially also affect key parameters of vector transmission. The present study investigated the consequences of changes in oocyst intensity on subsequent parasite development and mosquito survival. To address this
column9:
we experimentally produced different intensities of infection for Anopheles gambiae females from Burkina Faso by diluting gametocytes from three natural Plasmodium falciparum local isolates and used a newly developed non-destructive method based on the exploitation of mosquito sugar feeding to track parasite and mosquito life history traits throughout sporogonic development.

This dataset provides the code (for R software) and R data used for statistical analysis to estimate:

  • the effect of parasite density on parasite extrinsic incubation period ;
  • the effect of parasite density on mosquito survival ;
  • the infection prevalence and intensity in mosquito gut at 7 days post blood meal (dpbm) ;

This study assessed community readiness to address unhealthy food and beverage consumption in women of reproductive age in urban Ghana. The Community Readiness Model (CRM) assessed the stage of readiness of communities within Accra and Ho to address the consumption of unhealthy foods and beverages. In-depth individual interviews were conducted in 2018 with 24 key informants from various sectors across the two cities.

The CRM survey consists of 36 open questions addressing five readiness dimensions: community knowledge of efforts; leadership; community climate; knowledge of the issue and resources.

Data available: 24 qualitative interviews transcripts (about 20 pages each)

column3:
Paul‡"Lien vers les données

Ce jeu de données rassemble les éléments méthodologiques et les résultats d'un atelier participatif de co-construction d'un système de surveillance et d'alerte précoce pour le moustique tigre (Aedes albopictus) en Occitanie.

L'atelier a été organisé par l'Observatoire Montpelliérain en Écologie et Évolution de la Santé (OMEES) et s'est tenu le 25 novembre 2025 dans les locaux de la Maison des Sciences et des Humanités (MSH SUD) à Montpellier.

Intitulé « Du besoin au prototype : co-concevoir un système de surveillance et d'alerte précoce pour le moustique tigre »
column4:
cet atelier a réuni une diversité d'acteurs concernés par cette problématique :

  • des chercheurs (IRD
column5: CIRAD
column6: INRAE)
column7: • des opérateurs de santé publique (ARS
column8: Santé publique France)
column9: • des opérateurs de la lutte anti-vectorielle (EID Méditerranée
column10: Altopictus)
column11: • des services de collectivités territoriales (Ville et Métropole de Montpellier)
column12:
• des représentants de la société civile (association Concise).

    • L'objectif de l'atelier était d'identifier les besoins des utilisateurs en matière d'informations spatio-temporelles sur la dynamique du moustique tigre
column13: et de définir  les modalités de restitution de ces informations afin qu'elles soient compréhensibles
column14:
accessibles et opérationnelles pour des publics non experts. Les résultats ont vocation à alimenter le développement d'un prototype de système d'alerte précoce opérationnel.

Microplastic concentrations (300-5000µm) in surface water and sediment were investigated in two estuaries in Central Vietnam: Thuan An Estuary (Thua Thien Hue Province) and Tra Khuc Estuary (Quang Ngai Province) in April 2021.

Microplastics in water were sampled using a stainless steel bucket and filtered through a plankton net (mesh size: 80 μm). Sediment sampling was implemented using a stainless steel sediment core sampler (diameter: 5 cm). Microplastics in the samples were treated by bio-enzyme and H2O2 30%
column5: density separated by NaCl
column6: and filtered through Whatman glass fiber filters (GF/A
column7: pore sizes of 1.6 μm). Microplastics were observed by a stereomicroscope and classified into groups of shape
column8: color
column9:
and size. Microplastic concentration was expressed as the number of particles per m3 (item m-3) for water and the number of particles per kilogram of dry-weight sediment (item kg-1) for sediments.

column3:
Hinda‡"Lien vers les données

This dataset contains additional supporting information related to the discovery of an alternative transcriptional target locus of the Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae (Xoo) TalC effector in the rice genome as described in a manuscript submitted for publication.

Xoo strains that cause Bacterial Leaf Blight (BLB)
column4: limit rice (Oryza sativa) production and require breeding more resistant varieties. Transcription Activator-Like Effectors (TALE) activate transcription to promote leaf colonization by binding to specific plant host DNA sequences termed Effector Binding Elements (EBE). Xoo major TALEs universally target susceptibility genes of the SWEET transporter family. TALE-unresponsive alleles of clade III OsSWEET susceptibility gene promoter created with genome editing confer broad resistance to Asian Xoo strains. African Xoo strains rely primarily on the major TALE TalC which targets OsSWEET14. Although the virulence of a talC mutant strain is severely impaired
column5: abrogating OsSWEET14 induction with genome editing does not confer equivalent resistance to African Xoo. To address this contradiction
column6: we postulated the existence of a TalC target susceptibility gene redundant with OsSWEET14. Bioinformatics analysis identified a rice locus named ATAC  composed of the INCREASED LEAF INCLINATION 2 (ILI2) gene and a putative lncRNA that are shown to be bidirectionally upregulated in a TalC-dependant fashion. Gain of function approaches with designer TALEs inducing ATAC sequences did not complement the virulence of a Xoo strain incapable of activating a SWEET gene. While editing the TalC EBE at the ATAC loci compromised TalC-mediated induction
column7: multiplex edited lines with mutations at the OsSWEET14 and the ATAC loci remained essentially susceptible to African Xoo strains. Overall
column8:
this work indicates that ATAC is a probable TalC off-target loci but nonetheless documents a first example of divergent transcription activation by a native TALE during infection.

This dataset is composed of two pieces of information related to the trancriptome of the Nipponbare rice leaf undergoing infection with Xoo:

- The inferred experimental transcriptome following bacterial inoculation.

- The gene-level expression analysis output in response to bacterial treatments.

In the last decade
column7: the prevalence of Trypanosoma brucei gambiense human African trypanosomiasis (HAT) has fallen and HAT has been targeted for elimination. At low disease prevalence
column8: integration of case finding into routine activities of peripheral health centres becomes crucial. However
column9: HAT case detection by the peripheral health system with limited resources requires adapted diagnostic tests and test algorithms. The objective of the DiTECT-HAT-WP2 study was to determine the diagnostic performance and cost of rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) performed on clinical suspects in peripheral health centres
column10: whether or not followed by serological and/or molecular tests on filter paper done at regional reference centres. DiTECT-HAT has been registered in clinicaltrials.gov under ID NCT03356665
column11: and is part of the DiTECT-HAT project
column12:
funded by EDCTP2-Horizon 2020- European Union.

The DiTECT-HAT-WP2 study was conducted in centres for diagnosis and treatment and in sites for serological screening in Guinea
column13: Côte d'Ivoire and DR Congo. In these centres and sites
column14: clinical suspects were tested with several commercially available RDTs for HAT. Clinical suspects with at least 1 RDT positive result
column15: 1° underwent parasitological examination and 2° blood collection on filter paper for reference analysis in trypanolysis
column17: ELISA and real-time PCR in the regional reference laboratory. If the reference laboratory tests and parasitological examinations were all negative
column18: the suspect was considered free of HAT. If at least 1 reference test was positive
column19: parasitological examinations were repeated at least twice at three months interval
column20:
unless trypanosomes were detected.

The deposited file contains the DiTECT-HAT-WP2 study protocol in French and English
column21: the informed consent form
column22: and metadata of DiTECT-HAT-WP2 in Guinea. DiTECT-HAT-WP2 data will be made available to qualified researchers upon request after signing a confidentiality agreement. Data requests may be sent to the coordinating investigator (for coordinates see study protocol

Cette opération de recherche correspond à une thèse produite sous le thème « Urbanisation en périphérie métropolitaine d'Abidjan
column3: secteur Port-Bouët Grand-Bassam (2014-2019) ». Elle a été soutenue publiquement le 30 Avril 2021 par ZUO ESTELLE DIATE à l'Institut de Géographie Tropicale de l'Université Félix Houphouët-Boigny (IGT-UFHB)
column4: Abidjan Côte d'Ivoire
column5: sous la direction de monsieur NASSA Dabié Désiré Axel
column6: Professeur titulaire. Les territoires étudiés sont ceux des communes de Port-Bouët (111 km²
column7: 338 000 habitants RGPH 2014) et de Grand-Bassam (12
column8: 41 km²
column9: 84 028 habitants
column10: RGPH 2014) situés dans la périphérie est du District d'Abidjan. La périurbanisation
column11: est un phénomène qui questionne de par l'ampleur de la dynamique de l'urbanisation aux portes des grandes métropoles comme Abidjan. Ce qui est encore plus surprenant
column12: c'est que cette périurbanisation est aux mains d'acteurs villageois et privés qui n'ont pas pour fonction l'organisation de l'espace appelée à répondre à une demande d'ordre métropolitaine. L'étude a montré que ce sont ces acteurs villageois
column13: civils et privés qui devancent l'Etat dans la production de la ville dans un contexte de paradigme néolibérale favorable à la valorisation du droit de propriété. L'espace de la périphérie sud est répond plus à la satisfaction des intérêts privés individuels qu'à la prise en compte de l'intérêt général qui est le but de l'aménagement public. L'étude décrit la démographie
column14: l'histoire
column15: la géographie
column16: la sociologie et la gouvernance de ces territoires. Les termes abordés sont : planification urbaine
column19: acteurs du développement urbain
column20: stratégies d'appropriations foncières des populations civiles et privées
column21: formes de mise en valeurs de l'espace urbain
column23: évolution
column25: conflits fonciers pour le contrôle de la terre qui est passée de biens collectifs au statut de biens marchands par le système de marché du contexte néolibéral. Les questionnements principaux sont les suivants : comment l'urbanisation en périphérie métropolitaine Sud-Est d'Abidjan s'opère- t- elle dans le contexte du paradigme actuel de la distribution des rôles entre l'Etat et les agents privés ? Quelle est la politique d'aménagement conduite par les institutions publiques dans la périphérie Sud-Est ? Quelles sont les stratégies de production urbaine développées par les acteurs privés et civils de l'urbanisation dans l'espace péri-métropolitain du Sud-Est ? Quels sont les effets urbains induits des formes actuelles de mise en valeur sur l'organisation de l'espace périphérique ?   Les données produites sont ici à minima citées mais si possible décrites et accessibles. Il s'agit : du Protocole de Recherche (projet

Qualitative community surveys were used to understand local features and dynamics. Cet ensemble de données comporte les documents utilisés pour l'apprentissage des modules NER (Named Entity Recognition) du projet MeDo (http://webmedo.msem.univ-montp2.fr/?page_id=12).

Les documents originaux ont été requêtés sur le web
column4:
convertis au format texte et nettoyés. Les fichiers ont été anonymisés en remplaçant les caractères ne correspondant pas aux annotations par des X. Les fichiers .txt correspondent aux fichiers anonymisés (encodage UTF-8).

L'annotation a été réalisée avec le logiciel Brat (http://brat.nlplab.org/index.html). Les fichiers .ann correspondent aux résultats de l'annotation manuelle sous Brat. Le guide d'annotation est aussi disponible au téléchargement (https://doi.org/10.23708/DAAKF1).

Les mots clés des requêtes
column5:
ainsi que les liens URL des documents originaux sont détaillés dans le fichier info.JSON joint à ce dépôt.
This dataset contains the documents used to train the NER (Named Entity Recognition) modules of the MeDo project (http://webmedo.msem.univ-montp2.fr/?page_id=228&lang=en).

The original documents have been scraped from the web
column6:

Ce(tte) œuvre est mise à disposition selon les termes de la Licence Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International."‡Integrative omics approaches reveal mechanisms of combined heat stress and extreme hypoxia tolerance in a Cerambycid beetle larva‡JAVAL
column3:
Marion‡"Lien vers les données
Atmospheric oxygen
column4: which is essential for energy metabolism
column5: can directly influence an animal's heat tolerance by affecting oxygen transport processes
column6: especially in those living in oxygen-poor environments
column7: such as inside plant tissues
column8:
underground or in aquatic environments.
Yet
column9: the interplay of oxygen and temperature are rarely studied together
column10:
limiting our ability to predict their combined effects on insect performance.

This study examines the tolerance of a large xylophagous cerambycid beetle Cacosceles newmannii to combined hypoxic and thermal stress using performance assays (duration of righting response) coupled with transcriptomic and metabolomic analyses.
Metabolomic profiling showed that most amino acids were downregulated in the body but upregulated in the haemolymph as stress increased. Transcriptomic profiles clustered primarily by temperature (25 °C vs 35 °C)
column11:
independent of oxygen level.
Consistently
column12:
both the number of differentially expressed genes and enriched GO terms were much higher between temperatures than between oxygen treatments.
Cacosceles newmannii appears capable of modulating its performance to reduce the energy costs and physiological damage induced by hypoxia. This suggests a high baseline hypoxia tolerance rather than a rapid plastic (induced) physiological hypoxia response
column13: probably due to the species' endophytic lifestyle. Conversely
column14: thermal stress leads to a predictable increase in metabolic activity but does not markedly affect performance
column15: triggering adjustments to maintain cellular functions while limiting the impact of stresses expected under conditions of high temperature
column17: our study highlights the distinct metabolic pathways mobilised to cope with hypoxic versus thermal stress
column18: emphasizing the importance of integrated approaches in understanding insect responses to environmental changes. These findings have significant implications for understanding the species' ecology
column19:
with applications for pest management and sustainable agriculture in the context of climate change.

This dataset contains performance data acquired from Cacosceles newmannii.
Performance was assessed under the different oxygen and temperature conditions both with and without an acclimation period of 6 days at the same conditions. It was estimated by measuring the time taken by larvae placed on their backs to turn over onto their ventral side. Larvae were subjected to all treatments in a random order
column20: first without the acclimation period
column21: and second after the 6-day acclimation period. Duration of righting was measured five times for each larva at each treatment. Larvae were given at least 5 days to recover between two treatments
column23: larvae were allowed to rest for at least 5 minutes between each of the 5 replicate trials. Larvae were individually placed in a custom-made chamber built on a peltier heating surface and air was directed into the chamber at the desired oxygen level using an overpressure funnel system. Larvae were given 2 minutes before they were gently pushed onto their back with a thin wooden stick
column24:
in order to i) limit the effect of handling stress and ii) allow the oxygen level to equilibrate after the chamber was briefly open. The time needed for a larva to turn on its ventral side again was recorded to the second.

Metabolomics data are published in the supplementary material of the associated article
column25: and transcriptomics data have been deposited in the European Nucleotide Archive (ENA) at EMBL-EBI under accession number PRJEB106986 (https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/browser/view/PRJEB106986) and will be available upon acceptance of the article."‡IRD; stress adaptation; insect physiology; metabolic pathways; Cacosceles newmannii; Biology

itself part of IBV's PhD project on the comparative phylogeography of Indo-Pacific sea-urchins of the genera Diadema and Echinometra.

METHODS
Briefly
column6: long-spined sea urchins were sampled throughout the Indo-Malay archipelago between July 2019 and November 2021. Gonad tissue was dissected and preserved in ethanol. Genomic DNA was extracted and a 1157-nucleotide long segment beginning at the 5' end of the mitochondrial cytochrome oxidase subunit I (COI) gene was amplified by polymerase-chain reaction according to Ivanova & Grainger's (2007) protocols. Amplicons were sequenced according to the Sanger protocol. Sequence chromatograms were verified under Chromas v. 2.6.5 (Technelysium
column7: Brisbane
column8:
Australia). All nucleotide sequences were deposited in GenBank (Clark et al. 2016) and allocated accession numbers OP310072 to OP310789. Nucleotide sequences were aligned using the ClustalW algorithm implemented in MegaX v. 10.0.4 (Kumar et al. 2018).

column5:
Dominique‡"Lien vers les données
Le programme ""Intelligences du savoir-faire le développement et capacités à l'intégration des nouveaux modèles éco-écologiques"" s'inscrit dans les courants croisés des Sustainability sciences et de l'écologie politique. Dans le cadre du renouvellement des approches scientifiques du développement durable dont le CEMOTEV et l'UMI Résiliences se font les porteurs
column6: nous proposons d'étudier les expériences des projets en éco-écologie du développement durable qui ont cours en Côte d'Ivoire. Ces projets innovants portent l'ambition de modifier les rapports entre l'économie de marché et l'économie de l'habiter sachant que pour le moment la première est surtout la créatrice durable des mises en danger et en vulnérabilité de la seconde.  Notre objectif est d'explorer l'adaptation aux réalités locales ivoiriennes du changement de paradigme proposé par la Banque mondiale pour le développement visant une prospérité partagée dans le contexte ivoirien. Pour le moment le mécanisme prévalent est celui de l'extraction de la plus-value financière qui bloque la plupart des initiatives innovantes des acteurs de la production locale sous des plafonds de verre
column7: et fait rétrograder l'intégrité et la pérennité des sociétés locales. Nous voudrions aussi tenter de mettre au jour et aider à évaluer les avantages et les nécessités d'une distribution minimale de la plus-value financière dans l'économie locale et de l'habiter.  Inventer les outils d'un dialogue transdisciplinaire radical pour faire émerger une intelligence de la  transition éco-écologique locale soutenable L'idée est de mettre au point des modes d'analyse et de documentation appropriés de façon à ce que la recherche ivoirienne puisse jouer son rôle de médiateur intellectuel public et de force de proposition auprès des décideurs comme de la société civile pour un plan de développement éco-écologique adapté tout à la fois à l'objectif d'une qualité sanitaire de l'alimentation et au repositionnement économique viable des forces disponibles pour cela.  Dans notre domaine de compétences
column8: trois tableaux nous semblent être à produire dans une démarche de formation et d'innovation par la recherche : - le tableau des conditions et des éléments d'adaptation de la transition éco-écologique; - le tableau des éléments de blocage et des solutions possibles à leur dépassement
column9: notamment ce qui relève des capacités gouvernementales d'encadrement structurel ; - le tableau des avantages collatéraux
column10: qui peuvent être des éléments argumentaires de taille pour obtenir l'adhésion politique et/ou les moyens financiers. Ce qui est intéressant c'est que ces diverses conditions
column11: facteurs et avantages peuvent relever selon le contexte autant des dimensions environnementales
column12: démographiques
column13: sociales et culturelles
column14: structurelles et politiques
column15: que des cadres de la réalisation de soi et des échanges interpersonnels. C'est en cela que cette démarche s'avère transdisciplinaire et demande de pouvoir solliciter les savoirs existants dans les multiples domaines de savoir dans une situation de développement. Il s'agit à la fois de constituer le contenu et de réfléchir à la forme de ces tableaux. Il faut donc mener une réflexion méthodologique innovante. Notre idée est d'initier et de constituer les modèles de tableaux à partir d'une première étude pilote des expériences d'agriculture biologique et de commerce équitable en Côte d'ivoire et d'intégration des produits qui en sont issus dans l'économie domestique
column16: à la fois localement en Côte d'ivoire et dans les pays vers lesquels les produits alimentaires ivoiriens participent à la chaîne alimentaire.  Pour mettre au point outils et tableaux
column17: nous proposons une étude pilote de la faisabilité d'une éco-écologie transitionnelle du cacao pour initier le protocole de formation par la recherche. Les modèles écologiques de production du cacao tels que la permaculture et la forêt alimentaire
column18: exploitent la mixité des cultures et la complémentarité entre végétaux. Concevoir une éco-écologie transitionnelle du cacao signifie de fait l'articuler avec celle du vivrier. De plus ces nouveaux modes de culture impliquent de recourir à un travail manuel et à haute intensité de main d'œuvre

column3:
Mathilde‡"Lien vers les données
Background: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in Enterobacterales is a growing concern in sub-Saharan Africa
column4: yet data remain scarce for Central Africa.   Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis (2008–2024) to estimate the prevalence of extended spectrum ß-lactamase-producing Enterobacterales (ESBL-EB) and AMR frequency across nine Central African countries. Systematic searching strategy and risk of bias assessment followed PRISMA guidelines. Prevalence or frequencies and 95% confidence intervals were estimated using a random effects meta-model with the Der Simonian and Laird method.   Results: Here we show an ESBL-EB pooled prevalence of 22% (41% for carriage and 10% in clinical samples) for the 24 selected articles assessing ESBL-EB. For the 26 selected articles assessing AMR frequency
column5: we find it is high for cotrimoxazole (63–81%)
column6: amoxicillin/clavulanic acid (43–63%)
column7: ciprofloxacin (24–33%)
column8: and remains lower for nitrofurantoin (12–18%) and amikacin (13–19%). We provide pooled estimates for alternative agents and highlight the near-total absence of data for WHO Reserve antibiotics
column9: including carbapenems
column10: newer β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitors
column11: and fosfomycin. Significant heterogeneity and high exclusion rate due to risk of bias reflect major diagnostic limitations in the region.   Conclusions: The high prevalence of AMR in Central Africa underscores the urgent need to strengthen antimicrobial susceptibility testing capacities
column12: restrict empirical use of key antibiotics
column13: and ensure equitable access to effective antimicrobials. Without coordinated investments for microbiology capacities scale-up
column14: prescription regulation
column15: and strategies for water

column3:
Marion‡"Lien vers les données
Tuta absoluta (Meyrick) (Lepidoptera
column4: Gelechiidae) is a destructive pest of tomato and many other solanaceous crops
column5: with very high economic impacts. Its invasion threatens food security in a large part of the globe
column6: in areas such as sub-Saharan Africa where the agricultural resilience has already been weakened by rapid human-induced changes due in particular to population growth
column7:
increased trade and global change.

This work aimed to investigate the diversity and genetic structure of 60 populations of T. absoluta using microsatellite markers
column8:
with a particular focus on sub-Saharan Africa.

NB: Loci with a mean estimate of null-allele frequency greater than 0.15
column9: significant heterozygote deficiencies after Bonferroni correction and a highly substantial inbreeding coefficient were not used in the analyses in the associated paper. column3: 2021‡Dada

column5:
Olusegun‡"Lien vers les données

The vulnerability of coastal territories has multiple dimensions
column6: making it difficult to assess. One way of collecting data about the vulnerability of coastal areas is to interview people living on the coast. These communities have information on the coastal phenomena that occur on their territory and on the evolution over time of the frequency
column7: intensity
column8: and speed of progression of the pressures and pulses affecting them. They are the first observers of these phenomena. They are also the ones who suffer the consequences first
column9: and who are most able to provide information on the impacts of coastal hazards on their territory. People living on the coast are also actors in their territory
column10: where they carry out activities and development practices. Coastal communities
column11: which are the focus of our survey
column12: can therefore play four roles in the vulnerability of coastal socio-ecosystems: observers
column13: impacted victims
column14: agents of degradation
column15: and agents of remediation. The consideration of these four roles is the basis for designing the current survey. The questionnaire is designed to be administered in several areas of the West African coastline. It is simple
column19: which are salinization
column20: coastal erosion
column21: flooding
column23: and oil pollution for cases in Senegal and Ghana. The second dimension that structured the questionnaire is an in-depth description of these major hazards. Thus
column24:
the questionnaire is designed as an appropriate tool for vulnerability assessment.

A pilot survey was conducted in the Anlo Beach community in the Western Region of Ghana to obtain information on risk perceptions. The survey targeted a sample of 156 respondents
column25: with each respondent being an adult member (generally husband or wife) of a given household. To get the sample of households
column26: a systematic sampling of every 5th household within the list of 740 enumerated households was carried out to ensure that the results that will be obtained from our survey will approximate what would have been obtained if the entire population had been surveyed. The interviews were conducted face-to-face in September and October 2021. Kobocollect software
column28: was used to conduct the interview. In most cases
column29:
responses provided to the questionnaire were used directly as binary or multi-modal variables.
