Content of the BMO

The Bulletin Mensuel des Observations have been published for the first time in 1910 under the direction of Georges Le Cadet
column4: director of the Service Météorologique de l'Indochine
column5: and later on by the following directors (Etienne Bruzon
column6: B. Guilmet
column7: A. Romer) until 1942. The collection recovered at Trappes comprises 119 issues (fascicles)
column8:
each corresponding to one month:

  • 1910: Jan
column9: Feb
column10:
Mar (3 fascicles)
  • 1925: Complete year (12 fascicles)
  • 1926: Jan-Oct (10 fascicles)
  • 1927: Complete year (12 fascicles)
  • 1928: Complete year (12 fascicles)
  • 1929: Complete year (12 fascicles)
  • 1930: Complete year (12 fascicles)
  • 1937: Complete year (12 fascicles)
  • 1938: Complete year (12 fascicles)
  • 1939: Jan-Jun (6 fascicles)
  • 1940: Complete year (12 fascicles)
  • 1941: Complete year (12 fascicles + 1 fascicle of supplement)
  • 1942: Jul (1 fascicle)

    • As can be seen
column11: the number of lacks is very high. There are particularly two big gaps: 15 years between 1910 and 1925 and 6 years between 1930 and 1937. In the current state of our research
column12:
it is still impossible to say whether these gaps are due to an incomplete collection of the Météo France in Trappes or to the fact that the BMO was not regularly published.

The second hypothesis seems more likely
column13:
as the BMO collection held at the BNF has fewer issues than that of Météo France but has approximately the same gaps; and as the first page of the December 1930 issue bears the following information: ""Publication of the BMO is suspended until further notice"". This terse form is not followed by any explanation.

Nevertheless
column14:
we would ask users of this database to let us know if they can provide any additional issues missing in the present dataset.

Content of the dataset

The present dataset content all the data of the collection of BMO recovered in Météo France Trappes.

column5:
Laurence‡"Lien vers les données
The rice yellow mottle virus (RYMV
column6: Sobemovirus RYMV) is one of the most devastating viral pathogens affecting rice production in Africa.The recessive resistance gene rymv1 encodes the eukaryotic translation initiation factor 4G isoform eIFiso4G1
column7: which acts as a susceptibility factor. Its interaction with the viral VPg protein is essential for infection
column8: and disruptive mutations in eIFiso4G1 are associated with resistance. While naturally occuring resistance alleles are predominantly found in the African cultivated species Oryza glaberrima
column9:
their use in breeding programs is limited by interspecific sterility barriers with the widely cultivated O. sativa.
CRISPR-Cas9 technology offers new oppportunities to introduce targeted mutations in elite genotypes
column10:
already enabling the generation of knockout (KO) or insertion-deletion (Indel) mutations in susceptibility genes involved in several plant-virus interactions. The aim of our study was to evaluate whether CRIPSR-Cas9 could facilitate the development of eIFiso4G1 variants associated with RYMV resistance and contribute to breeding elite rice lines with durable resistance.
Using the Kitaake genetic background
column11: we developed lines carrying: (1) KO mutations in eIFiso4G1 and its homologous copy
column12: eIFiso4G2
column13:
(2)Indel variants in the eIF(iso)4G1 region critical for interaction with the viral VPg protein.
These lines were evaluated under controlled conditions for resistance to diverse RYMV isolates and for morphological traits. This dataset presents the phenotypic evaluation results."‡IRD; rice; disease resistance; rice yellow mottle virus; RYMV; gene editing; translation initiation factor; Biotechnologies; Plant sciences; INRAE‡Experimental data‡"data_morphology.tab; data_resistance_BF1_isolate.tab; data_resistance_other_isolates.tab; methodology.pdf; ReadMe_10_23708_ZWRXNJ.txt; Rscript.R; Rscript_readme.txt; Vocabulary.txt"‡‡‡‡‡‡‡‡

column3:
Juliette‡"Lien vers les données
High species richness is observed in certain shrublands on infertile substrates. Mineral nutrients are likely to be the primary limiting resources in these ecosystems
column4: and below-ground plant interactions may be crucial to understanding their diversity. Using ionomics
column5:
we investigated whether there were nutritional variations between plant species that coexist in a shrubland located in an edaphically extreme environment.

We set up a 20 m × 20 m plot in a diverse shrubland (“maquis”) on ultramafic (infertile) substrate
column6:
in which we sampled all 475 plants taller than 1 m and characterized their ionome (22 elements).

In our study
column7: 37 species were identified in the plot
column8: representing all major forms of mycorrhizal symbioses
column9: as well as nitrogen-fixing plants
column10: cluster rooted and parasitic plants. Notably
column11: both nickel hyperaccumulating and manganese hyperaccumulating species were present. Hypervolume approaches were used to assess ionome overlap among the nine most abundant species
column12: with the results revealing limited overlap. Moreover
column13: it was observed that the rarest species in the plot also had the most functionally distinct features."‡IRD; New Caledonia; Shrubland; Ecosystems and natural habitat; INRAE‡‡"Ionomic_data_shrubland_New_Caledonia_data_dictionary.txt; Ionomic_data_shrubland_New_Caledonia.tsv"‡‡‡English‡‡166°49'59.7”‡‡22°11'00.7”‡

column2: anthropologie du vieillissement et de la maladie (2022-2024)‡Taverne
column3: Bernard‡Taverne
column4:
Bernard‡"Lien vers les données

Le vieillissement de la population est un phénomène qui se développe au plan mondial mais aussi en Afrique sub-saharienne. Il s'accompagne de l'apparition de maladies non transmissibles qui deviennent l'un des problèmes majeurs de santé publique. Grâce à l'efficacité des traitements ARV (antirétroviraux)
column5: vieillir avec le VIH devient une expérience de plus en plus fréquente
column6: générant des questions sur l'évolution des capacités fonctionnelles
column7: les relations inter-générationnelles et intra familiales
column8:
le double stigma du VIH et de l'âge.

L'OMS a défini un concept de « vieillissement réussi » et préconise
column9: pour y parvenir
column10: des interventions adaptées aux personnes et à leur environnement. En Afrique sub-saharienne où les structures de prise en charge gériatriques sont rares et la couverture maladie quasi inexistante
column11:
les personnes âgées vivant avec le VIH (PAVVIH) sont particulièrement vulnérables sur le plan médical et social.

En 2020
column12: un programme financé par Expertise France « Vieillir avec le VIH au Sénégal et au Cameroun (VIHeillir) »
column13: vise à intégrer la prise en charge de certaines comorbidités dans les consultations VIH et à proposer des stratégies d'accompagnement médico-social à long terme. La recherche anthropologique proposée
column14: « Grand Age et VIH »
column15: accompagnera ce programme
column16:
pour orienter ses interventions.

L'objectif général est de décrire et analyser l'expérience et les perceptions du vieillissement chez des personnes très âgées vivant avec le VIH au Cameroun et au Sénégal. Les questions porteront sur : le vécu du vieillissement avec le VIH et les co-morbidités ; le vécu des situations de dépendance physique et psychique ; les modalités d'adaptation des structures de santé ; les répercussions de l'épidémie de Covid-19 sur les PAVVIH ; les spécificités de genre liées au vieillissement avec le VIH. La recherche se déroulera à Yaoundé et à Dakar. Elle concernera les personnes de plus de 70 ans
column17: particulièrement touchés par les problèmes de santé et sociaux liés au vieillissement. Des entretiens individuels semi-directifs et de groupes seront menés auprès de 80 PAVVIH et leurs familles
column19: des associations
column20:
des institutions nationales de santé.

Dans une perspective anthropologique
column21: cette recherche doit permettre
column23: de contribuer à la production de connaissances anthropologiques actualisées sur le vieillissement
column25:
la dépendance; la fin de vie en contexte africains.

Les méthodes classiques en anthropologie seront utilisées avec des entretiens semi-structurés
column27: des observations dans les lieux de soins
column28:
en milieu communautaire et dans les lieux de vie des participants à la recherche. Les entretiens seront menés selon l'approche de trajectoire de vie (life course) pour analyser le vécu de la transition vers le statut de « personne âgée » et des situations de dépendance.

Cette approche permettra de comprendre les changements individuels
column29:
sociaux et politiques en relation avec ce passage générationnel. L'analyse des histoires de vie nous permettra de dégager les événements et les facteurs qui déterminent un vécu « réussi ».

L'étude concernera 70 personnes âgées (50 au Cameroun et 30 au Sénégal)
column31: vivant avec le VIH. Nous rechercherons une diversité de situations : vie au domicile ou dans une structure
column33: dans une situation (ou non) de dépendance
column34: ayant encore une activité ou en incapacité fonctionnelle
column35:
traitées (ou non) pour des co-morbidités. La moitié des sujets seront des femmes.

Parallel evolution provides among the strongest evidence of the role of natural selection in shaping adaptation to the local environment. Yet
column23: mode and tempo of the process of parallel evolution remains broadly debated and discussed in the field of evolutionary biology. In this study
column24: we harness the temporal resolution of paleogenomics to understand the tempo and independence of parallel coastal ecotype adaptation in common bottlenose dolphins (Tursiops truncatus). For this
column25: we generated <b>whole genome re-sequencing data from subfossil dolphins (8
column27:
626 years BP) originating from around the formation time of new coastal habitat and compared them with data from contemporary populations.

Under the new oneCGIAR 2030 strategy
column4: the CGIAR has been undertaking an integrated land-food-water systems research transformation
column5: to deploy sustainable agricultural innovations  at a faster rate for smallholder agriculture in the developing countries. France has been supporting this CGIAR reform
column6: with a France-CGIAR action plan signed in February 2021
column7: along three joint priority development themes: adapting and mitigating against climate change (theme coordinated by IRD)
column8:
nutrition and agroecological transformation of farming and food systems in the South.

Under the climate change theme
column9: an inception 2 Degree workshop was co-organized by CGIAR and IRD in 2019. Six collaboration themes were discussed: One health; adapting to multiple stresses; climate security in the MENA region; resilient and adaptive water systems; securing Asian mega deltas; circular bioeconomy. For more
column10:
go to “BRIDGE_Origin_2019-France-CG-dialogue-integrated-climate-action”.

Several France CGIAR collaborations emerged from this workshop including:

  • A literature review « Agroecology and climate change: a case study of CCAFS research programme » published in 2020 (Andrieu N.
column11: Kebede Y.
column12:
2020).
  • A BRIDGE concept note was submitted to the DEvelopment Smart Innovation through Research in Agriculture (DESIRA) on low-cost digital decision tools to improve water efficiency and cli-mate resilience of legume-based systems. While not successful due to tight deadline
column13:
its unique inter and transdisciplinary approach has attracted positive feedback for donors and research partners.
  • DESIRA project “Strengthening the evidence base for a climate resilient and low-carbon small-holder agriculture through agroecology in Latin America” developed and launched in 2020
column14:
with the participation of IRD and CIRAD teams.

One promising area of climate change research collaboration between France and CGIAR is the co-creation of holistic climate and water smart decision-support systems. Data revolution and more integrated model-based approaches could help water and agriculture stakeholders better evaluate the potential impact and trade-offs of various climate adaptation options to select locally adapted
column15:
robust solutions for a much needed sustainable and inclusive transformation of the agriculture and food sector.

This trans- and interdisciplinary approach is called BRIDGE for co-Building Resilient climate and water smart farming systems with Interdisciplinary and Integrated models and multi-actor Decision and chanGE platforms. For more details about BRIDGE
column16:

L'ORSTOM

column2: NE Brazil): Insights from cosmogenic nuclides and geomorphic surfaces‡Siame
column3:
Lionel‡Oliveira José‡"Lien vers les données

These text and Excel files provide supplementary information for the paper entitled ""Relief inversion and denudation dynamics in a semi-arid landscape (Araripe Plateau
column4:
NE Brazil): Insights from cosmogenic nuclides and geomorphic surfaces"".

The objective of this dataset is to present all the analytical results that support all the conclusions included in this paper. The Araripe Plateau
column5: a relict landscape in the semi-arid northeastern region of Brazil
column6:
provides a unique setting to investigate sediment dynamics and long-term denudation rates using \textit{in situ}-produced cosmogenic nuclides (\(\ce{^{10}Be}\) and \(\ce{^{26}Al}\)).

In this study
column7: we analyze bedrock and modern river sediment samples to determine denudation rates
column8: evaluate sediment mixing
column9:
and assess the landscape's response to erosional processes.

Our results indicate that
column10: while bedrock samples largely reflect a state of cosmogenic secular equilibrium
column11: river-borne sediments exhibit significantly lower $\frac{\ce{^{26}Al}}{\ce{^{10}Be}}$ (Al-Be) ratios
column12: pointing to a mixing process between freshly eroded material and recycled sediment from buried sources in the alluvial plain. Denudation rates in the Araripe region are low ($\leq$ 20~m~Myr$^{-1}$) and show limited variability across different slopes and precipitation levels
column13: consistent with other slow-tectonic settings like the Anti-Atlas in Morocco. However
column14: differential denudation between the sedimentary rocks of the Araripe Basin and the crystalline basement rocks primarily controls relief evolution
column15: driving the process of topographic inversion along the Ceará and Pernambuco slopes. In the Parnaíba basins
column17: watersheds developed in Paleozoic sedimentary rocks exhibit notably higher erosion rates
column18: indicating faster landscape evolution in these areas. A speculative analysis of the geomorphic surfaces around the Araripe Plateau suggests long-term denudation rates of 3–5 m~Myr$^{-1}$
column19: consistent with our cosmogenic nuclide-derived rates. These findings indicate a stable landscape with low relief
column20: where tectonic activity has minimal influence
column21: and lithological factors play a dominant role in controlling erosional patterns. The differential denudation rates across the plateau
column22: supported by chi metrics and the spatial distribution of lateritic covers
column23: provide insights into the ongoing evolution of this relict landscape and potential future drainage rearrangements. Our study underscores the complexity of sedimentary processes and the importance of combining cosmogenic nuclide analysis with geomorphic context to understand the interplay between erosion
column25: and landscape stability. Using a two-source mixing model
column26: we estimate that up to 84\% of river sediment in some watersheds originates from previously buried material. This trend correlates with the extent of the cumulative drainage network
column27: suggesting that longer transport pathways enhance sediment mixing. The apparent burial times inferred from Al-Be ratios also show a negative correlation with the fraction of freshly eroded material
column28: highlighting the role of deep-seated sediment contributions in shaping the cosmogenic signal. This integrated approach provides a refined perspective on sediment dynamics and denudation in slowly eroding landscapes
column29:
with broader implications for interpreting cosmogenic nuclide data in similar settings worldwide.

  • File Name description:
    1) for rivers: R_Basin_River_M_XX: virtual station (VS) from M mission located in a basin (Congo) on a river from XX (where M and XX represents respectively the satellite mission and the distance from VS to the river outlet). R stands for the river product.

  • File description:

    • The header in comment (#):
    • Lat stands for the latitude of the VS
    • Lon stands for the longitude of the VS
    • Mission_Track stands for the satellite mission and the ground track of the satellite.
    The data is provided in a table format where:
    • First column represents the date.
    • Second column represents the time.
    • Third column: represents the satellite-derived surface water elevation in meter at each satellite pass.
    • Fourth column: represents the uncertainty of the satellite-derived surface water elevation in meter at each satellite pass.

column3:
Stéphanie‡"Lien vers les données

This study was aimed to compare the accuracy and precision of portion estimation with a food atlas and with salted replicas relative to weighed records (the reference). The food portion photography atlas had been developed prior to this data collection with age-appropriate portion sizes for 11 foods frequently consumed by children (sizes for 6-11-month- and for 12-23-month-old children) and women of childbearing age in Ouagadougou
column4:
the capital of Burkina Faso.

Our data were collected through a survey in 2021 on a sample of 67 women with their children
column5:
35 aged 12-23 months and 33 aged 6-11 months. The provided dataset contains estimates of portion sizes of food consumed the previous day by the participants using two different estimation tools. Data records were anonymized by removing direct and indirect identifiers.
The analysis of the data collected resulted in the published article ""Estimation of food portion sizes in women of childbearing age and young children in Ouagadougou (Burkina Faso) using a food photography atlas and salted replicas: comparison with weighed records"".

The detailed protocol of the study was approved by an ethics committee in Burkina Faso
column6: the Ethics Committee of the Research Institute in Health Science (N° 29-2019/CEIRES). After explaining the study purpose and implementation to the participants
column7: and giving them an information note
column8:
we asked them to sign an informed consent form.

column3:
Marine‡"Lien vers les données
We conducted a comprehensive investigation of large low-recombining regions in cultivated populations of pearl millet
column4:
an outcrossing diploid African cereal. We adopted a population genomic approach based on the analysis of single nucleotide polymorphisms.

The repository contains the VCF files generated for three datasets: a Senegalese (126 accessions
column5: exome sequencing
column7: 018 SNPs)
column8: an African (125 wild and cultivated accessions from Western
column9: Central and Eastern Africa
column10: whole genome sequencing
column13: 513 SNPs) and a temporal dataset (377 accessions from 1976 and 2016
column14: exome sequencing
column16:
672 SNPs).

We studied seven large regions ranging from 5 to 88 Mb. These regions exhibit an excess of heterozygotes
column17: a distinctive hallmark of overdominance. They displayed a tendency towards a higher diversity and ratio of non-synonymous and deleterious variants. A total of 17% of the cultivated pearl millet genome exhibits a local population structure suggestive of overdominance or possibly pseudo-overdominance. Our empirical results contribute to the accumulation of knowledge that will enhance our understanding of the potential role of overdominance or pseudo-overdominance in maintaining genetic diversity

column3: Lucie‡Penicaud
column4:
Juliette‡"Lien vers les données

These data were collected in the frame of Juliette Pénicaud's PhD at LEGOS (Toulouse
column5: France)
column6: whose subject is the model-based study of dynamics and hydrosedimentary processes in the riverine estuary and in the plume of the Red River
column7: East Sea of Vietnam. The general objective of the thesis is to develop and deepen the expertise in terms of modelling and observation of the estuary and river plumes in the Haiphong area in order to reproduce their dynamics and to analyse the hydrosedimentary processes on the basis of numerical models
column8: benefiting for their calibration and validation from all archive or future data
column9: in situ or satellite. Supervision of the PhD: Sylvain Ouillon & Marine Herrmann (IRD
column10: LEGOS)
column11: with the strong involvement of Vu Duy Vinh (IMER
column12: VAST
column13:
Haiphong).

The Red River brings to the Gulf of Tonkin considerable quantities of fresh water (3700 m3/s) and suspended matter (45 Mt/year). This region
column14: whose coasts are very densely populated (approx. 30M inhabitants in the Red River delta)
column15: is subject to variability factors of different origins (atmospheric
column19: from extreme events (cyclones) to climate change (sea level rise is particularly sensitive in the delta region).  In estuaries
column20:
less dense freshwater flows over marine salt water with great variability in space and time.

As a continuation of this work
column21: new projects are currently starting on the analysis of river plumes in the Haiphong area (Cam Bach Dang and Van Uc rivers): <UL> <LI>Plume dispersion analysis based on monitoring of Lagrangian drifters (Alexei Sentchev
column23: ADCP) <LI>Concomitant measurements of suspended particles (turbidity
column25: grain size distribution) <LI>In-situ characterization of the spatial and temporal variability of suspended matter (in-situ measurement of grain size distribution
column27:
long-shore and cross-shore variability)

The Van Uc River is a typical distributary of the Red River
column28: with rice crops all along the banks that require water and construction of dikes for flooding protection
column29: and with constant river bed dredging for navigation purposes. The Van Uc estuary is located next to Haiphong city (third largest city of Vietnam). It varies in width from ~350 to ~450 m. The Van Uc River is ranked at the third position of the Red River system in terms of both water (14.5% of the total water discharge from the Red-Thai Binh system to the Gulf of Tonkin) and sediment discharge (14.4% of the total sediment flux of the Red-Thai Binh River to the Red River coastal area. Van Uc river is influenced by a strong seasonal river signal associated with the monsoon. Indeed
column30:
the wet summer season (June to September) accounts for 85-95% of the total annual rainfall in the region.

In-situ measurement campaigns were carried out to study estuarine processes. Field surveys have been performed in the Van Uc estuary and in the close coastal plume at different stages of the wet season
column31:
always in spring tides. Two sampling strategies have been employed. The first strategy consisted in longitudinal transects of around 15 to 20 km in the estuary performed at various tide levels. Stations were spaced around 2 km and efforts were made to bring closer together the stations where important turbidity was measured and when we discovered rapid change in salinity. The transects were made in around 3h and performed in both senses (from upstream to downstream and opposite). Transect stations extend from 13 km upstream to 9 km downstream the mouth.

The second measurement strategy consisted in a fixed station near the mouth of the river
column32: in order to follow the evolution of the same part of the estuary over a specific tide period.  During these transects and fixed stations we measured continuously the current velocity Acoustic Doppler Current Profiler (ADCP). At each station
column35: density and turbidity were measured using a profiling CTD Compact. Another CTD
column37: was used to perform instant profiles and sharpen the transects strategy
column38:
i.e. adapting better the evolution in salinity.

Land degradation constitutes a significant challenge in the countries along the Great Green Wall. Substantial efforts have been directed towards implementing Sustainable Land Management (SLM) practices aimed at combating land degradation.

In this context
column4: the dataset ""Degraded land reclamation actions in 4 countries along the Great Green Wall"" is a contribution to the effort (i) to collect
column5: centralize and describe degraded land reclamation actions (DLRAs) and (ii) to identify the different stakeholders in the countries along the Great Green Wall. It concerns 4 countries: Niger
column6: Burkina Faso
column7:
Ethiopia and Senegal.

Seven main steps for the data collection process regarding DLRAs were necessary for the creation of a spatially referenced data table that ensures traceability of DLRAs and enables them to be monitored.

Beyond geolocation
column8: three other monitoring criteria were considered during the data integration process: the description of DLRA
column9: details about implementation
column10:
details about stakeholders involved.

The dataset corresponds to what was made available by the countries using the same collection method. It has to be said that this dataset has yet to be completed for each country. It highlights disparities between countries in terms of data completeness. Despite the supposed lack of data and the disparities between countries
column11: the work has made it possible to define and describe a data collection method that can be applied in all other GMV countries. It define the information that is essential for the traceability and monitoring of a land recovery action. Finally
column12: it has made it possible to compile an initial substantial dataset on actions to reclaim degraded land
column13: the players involved and the areas concerned. This dataset
column14: which needs to be expanded
column15: forms a solid basis for further data collection in the 4 countries

column20: taxonomic order

column26: or relevant phenotypic traits). Cryopreservation metadata document the date of stabilisation

column29: storage at −150 °C)

column). Stock verification and quality control status are recorded to ensure traceability and integrity of stored material.

In the last decade
column5: the prevalence of Trypanosoma brucei gambiense (Tbg) human African trypanosomiasis (HAT) has fallen and HAT has been targeted for transmission interruption by 2030. To achieve this goal
column6:
disease surveillance remains essential and is based on the identification of cases after screening carried out in the field with a serological test.

The SpeSerTryp study is a prospective evaluation of the specificity of serological tests for the diagnosis of HAT in Côte d'Ivoire and Guinea. Since the prevalence of HAT is low in these countries
column7: sensitivity will not be assessed. The main objective is to evaluate and compare the specificity of 5 serological field tests: Bioline HAT 2.0 (Abbott
column8: South Korea)
column9: HAT Sero-K-SeT & HAT 2.0 Sero-K-SeT (Coris BioConcept
column10: Belgium)
column11: TDR DCN (USA)
column12: and the CATT/T.b. gambiense (Institute of Tropical Medicine Antwerp
column13: Belgium). The secondary objectives are: 1° to assess the specificity of the 5 serological field tests according to malaria status; and 2° to compare the performance of immunological laboratory tests (trypanolysis
column14: ELISA/T.b.gambiense
column15: giELISA) and molecular laboratory tests (Trypanozoon RT-qPCR multiplex
column16:
SNP RT-qPCR and SHERLOCK) as reference tests to differentiate false positive subjects to serological field tests from subjects who had contact with Tbg. Specificity values will be determined against a composite reference test consisting of the most sensitive HAT parasitological methods.

SpeSerTryp is a prospective study that will take place in the endemic health districts of Bonon and Sinfra in Côte d'Ivoire and in the endemic health districts of Forécariah
column17: Dubréka and Boffa in Guinea. In each country
column18: 500 participants will be actively recruited by mobile teams. The inclusion criteria are: Age greater than or equal to 10 years and obtaining signed informed consent. The exclusion criteria are: Severe anemia preventing blood sampling; serious illness preventing the obtaining of informed consent and participation in the study; or history of HAT. After obtaining informed consent
column19: a venous blood sample will be taken (6 mL). This specimen will be used to carry out the 5 serological tests for HAT
column20: the RDT for malaria
column21: and for participants with at least one of the 5 serological tests positive
column22: parasitological confirmation of HAT and molecular and immunological laboratory tests. The duration of participant recruitment is estimated to be around 30 days
column23:
while laboratory analyses will take about 6 months. HAT cases identified during the study will be treated in accordance with current national guidelines.
This study is conducted in accordance with the protocol
column24: the current version of the Declaration of Helsinki
column26:
and all national legal and regulatory requirements.

column2:
Jérémy‡Jérémy T Campillo‡"Lien vers les données

Loiasis
column3: a disease caused by the parasite Loa loa impacting approximately 10 million people in Central Africa
column4:
causes transient angioedemas called Calabar swellings and eye worm episodes.

Treatment is challenging
column5: particularly in regions where onchocerciasis
column6: another type of filariasis
column7:
is also prevalent.

We tested a new kind of test that can detect both diseases at once and compared its performance with a previously available test for loiasis. We took blood samples from 971 people living in an area of Congo where loiasis is endemic.

Out of the participants
column8: 35.4% had L. loa pre-larvae in the blood – known as microfilariae
column9:
and 72.0% had experienced loiasis-related signs.

The new test demonstrated promise in detecting the disease
column10: albeit with some likelihood of false positives. Additionally
column11: its performance varied according to the density of microfilariae in the blood. While the results exceeded expectations
column12: further testing is essential to ensure its reliability. If validated
column13: this test could prove instrumental in diagnosing both loiasis and onchocerciasis
column14:
offering a valuable tool for public health interventions in affected regions.

The Millennium Coral Reef Mapping Project provides thematic maps of coral reefs worldwide at geomorphological scale. Maps were created by photo-interpretation of Landsat 7 and Landsat 8 satellite images.

Maps are provided as standard Shapefiles usable in GIS software. The geomorphological classification scheme is hierarchical and includes 5 levels. The GIS products include for each polygon a number of attributes. The 5 level geomorphological attributes are provided (numerical codes or text). The Level 1 corresponds to the differentiation between oceanic and continental reefs. Then from Levels 2 to 5
column4: the higher the level
column5: the more detailed the thematic classification is. Other binary attributes specify for each polygon if it belongs to terrestrial area (LAND attribute)
column6: and sedimentary or hard-bottom reef areas (REEF attribute).  Examples and more details on the attributes are provided in the references cited. The products distributed here were created by IRD
column7:
in their last version.

Shapefiles for 245 atolls of the Pacific Ocean as mapped by the Global coral reef mapping project at geomorphological scale using LANDSAT satellite data (L7 and L8). The data set provides one zip file per country or region of interest. Global coral reef mapping project at geomorphological scale using LANDSAT satellite data (L7 and L8).

This dataset gathers geographical information for the distribution sites of Quercus ilex subsp. rotundifolia in Tunisia in 2020
column4:
as well as climatic data and seed morpho-physiological traits associated with the 18 main populations identified.
In order to identify adaptations to local environments that may reflect ecological strategies for stress avoidance and seed survival
column5: we tested whether seed functional traits vary
column6: in Quercus ilex subsp. rotundifolia
column7: along climatic gradient within its southern range of distribution
column8: at the edge of arid areas
column9:
in Tunisia. An updated survey on the distribution of Q. ilex in Tunisia allowed to characterize the bioclimates of the main populations.
We have measured variations for seed morphological traits
column10: desiccation sensitivity level
column11: germination and shoot emergence rates under controlled conditions
column12:
among 18 populations of Q. ilex sampled over the entire Tunisian distribution of the species. We have explored the relationships between observed variations in seed traits and climate factors.

column8:
but they all suffer from important bias. One bias of significance for us is the data origin: most datasets are composed of data coming from developed countries.

Facing this situation
column9: and the need of data with local context in developing countries
column10: we try here to adapt common data generation process to inclusive data
column11:
meaning data drawn from locations and cultural context that are unseen or poorly represented.

We chose to replicate MS COCO's data generation process
column12:
as it is well documented and easy to implement. Data was collected from January to April 2022 through Flickr platform.

This dataset contains the results of our data collection process
column13:
as follows :
  • 23 text files containing comma separated URLs for each of the 23 geographic zones identified in the UN M49 norm. These text files are named according to the names of the geographic zones they cover.
  • Annotations for 400 images per geographic zones. Those annotations are COCO-style
column14:
and inform on the presence or absence of 91 categories of objects or concepts on the images. They are shared in a JSON format.
  • Licenses for the 400 annotations per geographic zones
column15:
based on the original licenses of the data and specified per image. Those licenses are shared under CSV format.
  • A document explaining the objectives and methodology underlying the data collection

The calibration procedure involves a peristaltic pump and a precision weighing under variable intensity ranging from 10 to 800mm/h. A calibration curve was fitted for each gage. column3:
Claire‡"Lien vers les données

Dataset description

This dataset consists of environmental variables describing the urban landscapes over metropolitan Montpellier
column4: France. Two very high-resolution Pléiades satellite images
column5: acquired on November 2 and 6
column7: were used to derive these environmental variables. Orthorectified Pléiades images include a 50cm-resolution panchromatic band and four 2m-resolution multispectral bands (blue
column8: red
column9: green
column10:
near-infrared). A mosaic of the two images was made using Orfeo ToolBox (OTB) software (v. 8.1.2) to create a complete image of the Montpellier metropolitan area.

Spectral indices :

Spectral indice were obtained from multispectral Pléaides images by the following equations: Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) and Brightness Index (BI) were computed using the formula NDVI = (NIR - RED) / (NIR + RED) and BI =√(RED2+NIR2)
column11: where ""NIR"" refers to the reflectance measured in the near-infrared band and ""RED"" corresponds to the reflectance measured in the red spectral band. A threshold was applied to the NDVI (> 0.2) to retain only areas with significant vegetation cover. For the BI
column12:
values above 500 were selected to obtain an urban layer. We used the NDVI vegetation layer to reclassify the areas classified as buildings by the BI but where there actually was vegetation. Processing was carried out using the QGIS software v. 3.16.

Textural indices :

Textural indices were extracted from the panchromatic Pléaides images and the spectral indices NDVI and BI using the FOTOTEX algorithm (Teillet et al.
column14: 2024) to characterize the structure of urban types within the study area. In FOTOTEX
column15: Fourier transforms combined with principal component analyses (PCA) convert the textural information within the image into a frequency signal and reduce it into three principal components (PC1
column17: representing distinct texture indices. Processing was carried out using python v. 3.7. FOTOTEX was applied with the block method to the panchromatic image (PAN) with an analysis window size of 201 pixels and to spectral indices (NDVI and BI) with a window size of 101 pixels (i.e.
column18:
202 meters).

Grid description : grid_MTP_complete_IS_IT.tif

This grid of 202m is composed of environmental variables characterizing the Montpellier metropolitan area

Example of application of this dataset in Biomod2 :

This grid of environmental layers describing urban landscapes was used in a species distribution model (SDMs) to calculate probabilities of larval presence Aedes albopictus in public spaces in Montpellier
column51: France. We used in-situ observations of potential breeding sites from operational services of EID-MED (Entente Interdépartementale pour la Démoustication du littoral Méditerranéen) made exclusively in the public domain (i.e. storm drain
column52:
telecom cable chambers). SDMs were implemented using the RStats biomod2 package v. 4.2-5-2. Biomod2 allows building and evaluating individual SDMs and combining them into an ensemble model. Here are the results of SDMs ensemble model which allows to obtain probabilities of presence of Aedes albopictus in public spaces in the Montpellier metropolitan area.

column3:
Thomas‡"Lien vers les données

Neglected and under-utilized species represent important genetic resources and untapped reservoirs of diversity to address global food and nutrition challenges. Characterizing the extent of their genetic diversity and making it available is of prime importance to their wider use and to develop collaborative research networks.

White fonio (Digitaria exilis (Kippist) Stapf) is a small cereal domesticated and cultivated in West Africa. It represents locally important food resources for smallholder farmers of arid and semi-arid lands.

Using an unprecedented number of 1539 genotyped landraces
column4:
we assessed fonio genetic diversity and population structure across its entire area of cultivation with simple sequence repeat markers (SSRs). Results are available through an open and user-friendly R shiny application on this link.

We provide here two files:
(1) the passport data of the white fonio collection containing information for the 2372 accessions (ID
column5: country
column6: project
column7: year of sampling
column8:
geographical coordinates and whether the accession was included or not in the genetic analyses).
(2) The SSRs data for the 1539 accessions successfully genotyped
column9: associated with passport information. The genotypes were obtained with 14 microsatellites markers. For each marker
column10:
the score of the two alleles are separated by ""/"". Missing genotypes are coded ""999/999"".

The GRDH and SLC Sentinel-1 products are processed using SNAP platform to extract the backscattering coefficient and the interferometric coherence. NDVI (normalized difference vegetation index) is computed from Sentinel-2 products
column4: available on Theia website. The soil measurements consist of surface roughness and surface and root zone soil moisture. Vegetation variables include canopy height

La cartographie des paysages est cruciale pour le suivi et la compréhension d'un large éventail de processus
column4: et pour relever des défis tels que l'aménagement du territoire
column5: l'évaluation de la biodiversité
column6: les interactions santé-environnement
column7: et le suivi/évaluation des impacts des politiques de gestion de l'environnement et des ressources. Afin de développer une ""reconnaissance de l'approche paysagère"" opérationnelle
column8:
nous devons développer des cadres méthodologiques et des outils dédiés à la caractérisation et à la cartographie des paysages.

Le Centre d'Expertise Scientifique (CES) Paysage du Pôle Théia
column9: auquel est rattaché ce projet TOSCA du même nom
column10:
a pour ambition de développer des produits et des services qui rendent robustes et transférables l'analyse des paysages dans une diversité de domaine nécessitant la prise en compte de l'activité humaine sur des milieux donnés et auprès des gestionnaires qui mobilisent déjà cet objet intégrateur en aménagement du territoire.

Ce projet consiste à développer une méthode automatisée de délimitation des Unités Paysagères (UP) à partir de Variables Essentielles Paysagères (VEP) issues des données Copernicus.



Ce premier volet du projet TOSCA vise à déterminer des méthodes pour délimiter les paysages. Une première méthode est donc proposée dans ce jeu de données.
Vous pourrez retrouver les éléments suivants :
- un schéma général récapitulatif de la méthode ;
- l'ensemble des données mobilisées avec une série chronologique MODIS NDVI (produit MOD13Q1 pendant la période 2016-2020) et de données Sentinel-2 (2019) ;
- la cartographie des unités paysagères finale réalisée avec la méthode sur la zone test choisie ;
- un poster scientifique présenté au congrès INTECOL-2022 (cf. publication connexe).

Long-term ecological data are of paramount importance to document the effects of global changes on biodiversity. The site of Bandia
column4: 70km southeast of Dakar in western Senegal
column5: has been the scene of numerous ecological studies since the 1970s. The « Bandia forest » was classified as such in 1933
column6: and submitted to a management plan in 1953 (i.e. subdivided in plots for firewood and timber exploitation / plots for reforestation
column7: together with a ban on livestock wandering). However
column8: this regulation was hardly respected
column9: and logging
column10: fuelwood collection
column11: poaching and wandering of livestock regularly occurred
column12: leading to a progressive degradation of the environment until 1990 when the site was erected as the first private natural reserve of Senegal. From there
column13: a 1500ha fenced area further extended to 3500ha has permitted the regrowth of trees / shrubs
column14: the reintroduction of large mammals and overall
column15:
the regeneration of a typical Sahelo-Sudanian tree savannah.

In the frame of projects led by researchers of the Institut de Recherche pour le Développement (IRD
column17: rodent populations were monitored at various periods using a capture-mark-recapture (CMR) protocol on trapping grids that yielded important datasets on population dynamics and ecology of the main species present. Among them
column18: the Guinea Multimammate Rat Mastomys erythroleucus proved to represent the dominant species of this community. Thus
column19: CMR data were collected on M. erythroleucus between November 1975 and March 1981 by Bernard Hubert and his collaborators (Hubert
column21: between January 1983 and October 1986 by Khalilou Bâ
column23: 1987; Duplantier & Granjon
column26: between January 1997 and April 2001 by Khalilou Bâ (Bâ
column27: 2002) and between June 2007 and June 2012 by Khalilou Bâ
column29:
Youssoupha Niang and Yves Papillon (see http://vminfotron-dev.mpl.ird.fr:8080/bandia2_2/index.htm).

The datasets corresponding to three of these periods are gathered here to facilitate their consultation and use. Raw data from the 1975-1981 period were not available. For the other periods
column30: the data include the identity of each individual animal captured with some of its biological attributes (sex
column31: weight at first capture of each session
column33: its exact date and point of capture (indicated by a trap-specific code) at each trapping occasion
column34:
and additional comments that may be of help to interpret the data.

This dataset provides information on life histories of a significant number of individuals from a population of one of the most important rodent species of the Sahelo-Sudanian bioclimatic belt. Population dynamics (including abundance
column38: breeding cycle and small-scale movements can be estimated from the data here presented. Combined with information on climate (and especially rainfall)
column39: vegetation or any other variable of interest
column40: they may serve to test hypotheses linked to interindividual relationships within populations as well as to the determinants of abundance cycles in rodents
column41:
including those related to global change consequences.

column3:
Julia‡"Lien vers les données

This study aimed to better understand the lived experiences of Ghana School Feeding Programme (GSFP) caterers in order to identify context-specific challenges and solutions around their food procurement and provisioning practices. In 2024
column4: a qualitative participatory photography study was conducted with school caterers working for the national school meals programme
column5: GSFP
column6:
in the Greater Accra Region.

Participants were asked to take photos of three set themes:
  1. Something that makes providing a school meal easy for you;
  2. Something that makes providing a school meal difficult for you;
  3. A solution or way that difficulty could be overcome.

In addition to these set themes
column7: participants worked in pairs to co-create two new study themes
column8:
and final themes were selected by group vote. Co-created themes included: stress experienced during food shopping and delayed GSFP payments (district 1); stressful transportation and lack of school kitchens (district 2); and limited access and affordability of food items (district 3).

Individual follow-up interviews were conducted with 19 participants and used their photographs as a guide to better understand caterers' daily lived experience.

The data available in this set includes open access to the photovoice interview guide and participant consent form. The data also includes integral transcripts from the 19 interviews with personal information redacted. This data is available upon formal request to the listed contact for a research project. A period of embargo is placed on the transcripts until at least January 2026."‡IRD; food environment; food procurement; school meals; photovoice; ghana; Nutrition and alimentation; INRAE‡‡"0_ASH.zip; 0_LAN.zip; 0_PRAM.zip; Consent Form_Photovoice.pdf; Individual interview guide_Photovoice.docx; ReadMe.txt"‡‡‡English‡-0.37970118876947334‡-0.014098811230526614‡5.737905289692142‡5.3741347103078585‡

This dataset is a shapefile representing the proportion of threatened endemic species (both plants and animals) in 247 countries along with associated environmental and socioeconomic drivers. The geographic coordinate system is World Geodetic System 1984 (EPSG: 4326). Information on a total of 65
column4: 125 endemic species including 27
column5: 294 globally threatened endemic species (55% threatened plant species
column6: 45% threatened animal species) was extracted from the IUCN Red List. The categories of threatened species used in the analyses included vulnerable (VU)
column7: endangered (EN)
column8: critically endangered (CR)
column9: extinct in the wild (EW) and globally extinct (EX). We calculated the proportion of globally threatened endemic species among the total number of assessed endemic species per country (Chamberlain et al.
column10: 2020).  Associated environmental socioeconomic regional correlates included: 1) Cropland: The proportion of each country covered by crops (including food
column11: fibre and fodder crops and pasture grasses) was determined based on a FAO global map with a resolution of 5 arc-minutes (von Velthuizen et al.
column12: 2007); 2) HANPP: The proportion of net primary production appropriated by humans (HANPP) by harvesting or burning biomass and by converting natural ecosystems to managed lands with lower productivity was derived for the year 2010 from Krausmann et al. (2013); 3) Delta HANPP: We also computed the increase in HANPP over the period 1962-2010 (Krausmann et al.
column13: 2013); 4) per area GDP: The per area gross domestic product (GDP
column14: in international $) was obtained by calculating the median value over each country of all 5 arcmin cells of a recently gridded GDP dataset (Kummu et al.
column15: 2018); 5) Human Footprint (HFP): The global terrestrial human footprint (HFP) is an index integrating the influence of built environments
column19: pasture lands
column20: roads
column21: railways
column22: and navigable waterways on the environment based on remotely-sensed and bottom-up survey information (Venter et al.
column23: 2016). We extracted from a 1 km resolution HFP map the median value over each country in 2009; 6) Delta HFP: We also calculated the increase in median HFP over the period 1993-2009 (Venter et al.
column24: 2016); 7) Invasive alien plants: The richness of invasive alien vascular plant species recorded in each country was compiled by Essl et al. (2019); 8) Invasive alien animals: The richness of invasive alien animal species was derived from the Global Register of Introduced and Invasive Species database (http://griis.org/ accessed on 27-6-2018); 9) Delta temperature: Based on decadal climate maps produced by the IPCC over the last century with a 0.5° resolution
column25: we calculated the median of the change in annual mean temperature (in °C) between 1901-1910 and 1981-1990 (Mitchell & Jones
column26: 2005); 10) Delta rainfall: The same for annual precipitation (in mm); 11) Velocity temperature: We also calculated the median velocity of climate change based on the formula from Hamann et al. (2015) to evaluate the distance (in °) over which a species must migrate over the surface of the earth to maintain constant temperature conditions; 12) Velocity rainfall: The same for precipitation; 13) Roadless areas: The median area of a roadless fragment (in km²) was calculated from the global map of roadless areas published by Ibisch et al. (2016); 14) Wilderness areas: The proportion of wildlands (categories ‘wild woodlands' and ‘wild treeless and barren lands') was calculated from the anthropogenic biome map of Ellis et al. (2010); 15) Protected areas: The proportion of protected areas was estimated from the IUCN's shapefile of World Database on Protected Areas (https://www.iucn.org/theme/protected-areas/our-work/world-database-protected-areas); 16) Conservation spending: The mean annual conservation spending of each country (in international $) was taken from Waldron et al. (2017) to quantify investment to mitigate biodiversity loss; 17) Completeness of biodiversity information: We used data on the estimated percentage completeness of species records in GBIF
column27: as assessed through comparison with independent estimates of native richness. Inventory effort indices available for vertebrates (Meyer et al.
column29: 2016) were merged into a single metric based upon an average weighted by estimated native species richness."‡IRD; Anthropocene; Biodiversity crisis; Conservation; Data limitation; Global change; Habitat loss and fragmentation; Invasive alien species; Sixth mass extinction; Geography

R scripts are licensed under a GNU General Public License v3.0."‡Modeling plant phenotypic plasticity and its underlying genetic architecture (France
column3: Alexandre‡Grondin
column4:
Alexandre‡"Lien vers les données

Phenotypic plasticity can contribute to crop adaptation to challenging environments. Plasticity indices are potentially useful to identify the genetic basis of crop phenotypic plasticity. Numerous methods exist to measure phenotypic plasticity.

Among those
column5: the ratio (also called the response coefficient; Bouslama and Schapaugh
column6: 1984) or the relative distance plasticity index (RDPI; Valladares et al.
column7: 2006) rely on pairwise comparison of phenotypic means between environments (e.g. non-optimal versus optimal growth environments for instance). The coefficient of variation (CV) was calculated as the standard deviation of the trait for a genotype in all environments divided by the genotype phenotypic mean across all environments (Francis and Kannenberg
column8: 1978). The Finlay-Wilkinson joint linear regression analysis corresponds to regression of the phenotypic mean of a trait for a given genotype against the averaged phenotypic means of all the genotypes in each studied environment (Finlay and Wilkinson
column9: 1963). It allows calculation of the slope (also called linear plasticity) that represents the sensitivity of the trait across the environments for a genotype
column10: and the mean square error from the regression (MSE
column11: also called non-linear plasticity) that describes the deviation of the measured phenotypic value from the predicted phenotype in a given environment. The AMMI model uses an analysis of variance to characterize the genotype and environment main effects
column12: and a principal component analysis to characterize their interactions (Gabriel
column13: 1978). Parameters of AMMI analysis
column14: such as the AMMI Zhang's Euclidean distance (D) which is the distance of the interaction principal component point (IPC) from its origin in space (Zhang and Xiang
column15: 1998; Xie et al.
column17: or the AMMI stability value (ASV) which considers the magnitude of the sum of squares of the first two IPC
column18: have also been used as phenotypic plasticity indices (Purchase et al.
column19:
2000).

This dataset contains plasticity indices (ratio
column20: RDPI
column21: CV
column23: MSE
column26: shoot biomass and water use efficiency on 254 maize genotypes grown under well-watered and water stress treatments in an environmentally-controlled phenotyping platform
column27: in four independent trials performed in different years and seasons (values for traits in each environment were published by Alvarez Prado et al.
column28: 2017). The scripts used for calculation of the plasticity indices
column29: for estimation of the genetic variance
column30: for identification of QTL associated with the plasticity indices using GWAS
column31:
to measure the contribution of the identified QTL to the genetic variance and to perform a step forward analysis to evaluate the efficiency of the analysis are provided. We also provide a Table comprehensively describing the equation used for calculation of the different plasticity indices. The ability QTL associated with plasticity indices to contribute to the genetic variance was compared to that of QTL associated with the phenotypic means.

This dataset includes collections of observations made on 242 date palms planted in a palm grove located in Damerdjog which is located on the south-eastern coastal zone in the Republic of Djibouti. The observable properties related to these collections of observations are as follows:
  • The number of dry pinnate leaves per year.
  • The number of green pinnate leaves emitted per year.
  • The diameter of the date palm stipe.
  • The length of the date palm stipe.
  • The data collection took place on several dates between June 2020 and April 2021.

The data collected correspond to 940 observations. The palm trees are georeferenced as well as the palm grove and two zones in the palm grove (irrigated/non-irrigated).

Contents of the BEI

The Bulletin économique de l'Indochine is a monthly review reporting on the economic development of Indochina in all sectors of activity
column4: and published from July 1898 to 1945 (283 volumes). The Bulletin's dual propagandist and utilitarian aims (to help European settlers in their various endeavors) meant that it published a large number of in-depth studies on the development of mines
column5: of transport network
column6: of trade
column7: of European and indigenous agriculture
column8: plantations
column9: industrial plants
column10: irrigation system
column11: etc. Many scientific and technical expertise of crops
column12: medicinal plants
column13: forest production
column14:
the quality of timber... are also published.

With the same aim
column15: BEI publishes numerous statistics and observation data
column16:
and specially daily meteorological data from the main meteorological stations of Indochina. It also contains numerous articles on the different climates of Indochina (see collection D in this database).

Contents of the dataset

This dataset contains all the daily meteorological data that have been published in the BEI.

From July 1898 to June 1904 : daily temperature and rain for 8 stations in the first year and up to 40 stations at the end of this period (See in the tree structure tab file named: 1. Standard tables of daily data).

From June 1904 to december 1905
column17: as the number of stations increased rapidly
column18: publishing all the data became complicated from an editorial point of view
column19: especially for a journal not dedicated to meteorology.  This explains why
column20: from issue no. 32 in August 1904 (data for June 1904)
column21: the tables changed form
column22: still providing daily data for rainfall
column23:
but for temperatures only maximum and minimum of the month with the days of these last data (See in the tree structure tab file named: 2. New tables).

From January 1906 to December 1916
column24:
no meteorological data are published in the BEI

From January 1917 to December 1922
column27: the BEI published the monthly diagram from the central observatory of Phu Lien
column29: rainfall
column33:
etc. (See in the tree structure tab file named: 3. Diagramm of Phu Liên).

From April 1932 to August 1937
column34: the BEI started to publish again meteorological observations but only monthly data of temperature
column35:
evaporation and rainfall (See in the tree structure tab file named: 4. Monthly data of the 1930's).

After 1937
column36:
no more meteorological data was published in the BEI.

See also in the tree structure tab
column37:
file 5 Special tables and graphs from different periods.

column3:
Alexandre‡"Lien vers les données

Rice (Oryza sativa L.) is a staple food for half of the world's population and its biofortification is a key factor in fighting micronutrient malnutrition. However
column4: harmful heavy metals tend to accumulate in rice grains due to soil and water contamination. Therefore
column5:
it is important to concomitantly improve beneficial micronutrients content and reduce accumulation of non-desirable metals in rice grain.

In this project
column6: we aimed at identifying new QTLs governing rice grain ion content. For this
column7: we measured ion content in brown and white grains produced by 184 sequenced genotypes from a Vietnamese rice collection grown in the field in Vietnam
column8:
and performed association genetics (GWAS).

Ion content was measured at the ionomic platform of the University of Nottingham using Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometers (ICP-MS) following a similar procedure to Danku et al (2013). ICP-MS analyses detected the presence of Mg
column9: P
column10: S
column11: K
column12: Ca
column13: Mn
column14: Fe
column15: Co
column19: As
column20: Rb
column21: Sr
column23: B
column27: Se and Pb fell below the quantification limit. These latest elements were consequently excluded for further analysis. On the 16 remaining elements
column28:
BLUEs per genotypes for each ion in BR and WR were calculated using StatgenSTA package considering a resolvable incomplete block design for the analysis.

BLUEs were further used for association genetics on ion content in white and brown grains using a previously described genotypic dataset composed of 21
column29: 623 SNP markers <a href=""https://doi.org/10.1186/s12870-014-0371-7""> (Phung et al
column31: 27 significant associations were identified and delimited into QTL regions associated with macronutrients such as phosphate
column33: micronutrients such as iron or zinc
column34:
or toxic heavy metals such as arsenic and cadmium.

The findings offer novel QTLs associated with grain ion content and candidate genes that are potentially valuable for breeding programs aiming at rice grain biofortification and reduction in toxic metal accumulation.

column3:

During a project focused on rice microbial biodiversity (root microbiome and pathogens)
column4: we performed a pluriannual and multi-site survey (see https://doi.org/10.23708/8FDWIE). Because microbial species may be shared between rice and surrounding wild plants
column5:
we chose to perform a small survey in 2017 in 6 rice fields.

This dataset describes obtained data. It corresponds to a list of wild plant species identified in the borders of six rice fields (from three geographical zones in western Burkina Faso) during a survey performed in 2017.

More specifically
column6: the observations and sampling were performed during one day in each geographical zone: 26/10/2017 (Bama: 17BM02 and 17BL02)
column7: 28/10/2017 (Karfiguela: 17KA01 and 17KA01)
column8: 01/11/2017 (Banzon: 17BZ11 and 17SZ07). The precise location of each of these six fields appears in https://doi.org/10.23708/8FDWIE (file: Data_MP_Field_Location_dataset.tab). Three fields correspond to irrigated production system (BM02
column9: BZ11
column10: KA01) while the three others (BL02
column11: SZ07
column12:
TG01) are in rainfed lowlands.

The observations were performed on two consecutive borders of each rice fields.

For each field and each wild plant species
column13:
the covering area was estimated: 0: absent; 0.5: rare; 1: covering area < 10%; 2: covering area between 10% and 20%; 3: covering area between 20% and 30%. This information corresponds to the columns 3 to 8 in the dataset.

In addition
column14: samples from the five more frequent plant species have been collected for each field for further analyses. This led to a set of 30 samples
column15:
and a total of 26 different species. The wild plant species sampled are annotated with ""X"" in the columns 9 to 14 in the dataset.

Direct and semi-direct estimations of leaf area (LA) and leaf area index (LAI) are scarce in dense tropical forests despite the importance of such measurements to calibrate remote-sensing products
column4: forest dynamics and biogeochemical models (e.g. Dynamic Global Vegetation Models). Extensive and destructive sampling of 61 trees belonging to 13 species spanning all diameter and wood density classes was performed in the semi-deciduous forest of southeastern Cameroon. For each tree
column5: all leaves were weighed
column6: counted for a subsample of branches and LA measured for 10-50 leaves. Allometric models were calibrated to allow semi-direct estimation at tree- and stand-levels
column7: based on forest inventory data (R²=0.7
column8: bias=21.2%
column9: error=39.5%)
column10: also on novel tree metrics allowed by remote-sensing like airborne light detection and ranging (R²=0.63
column11: bias=35.1%
column12: error=58.73). Using twenty-one 1-ha forest inventory plots
column13: stand-level estimations of LAI spanned from 4.42-13.99. Models produced at stand-level estimation may be considerably useful to climate-vegetation modelling and remote-sensing communities."‡IRD; leaf area; allometric models; tropical African broadleaved forests; INRAE‡‡‡‡‡‡‡‡‡‡

Bash and R scripts are licensed under a GNU General Public License v3.0."‡Leaf ion content in pearl millet (Senegal
column3: Alexandre‡Nakombo-Gbassault
column4:
Princia‡"Lien vers les données

Pearl millet (Pennisetum glaucum) is a nutrient-rich cereal crop cultivated in arid and semi-arid regions
column5: particularly in sub-Saharan Africa
column6: where it serves as a vital source of grain and fodder for millions of smallholder farmers. This crop is known to produce in hot
column7: dry climates and nutrient-poor soils
column8: making it a strategic crop for enhancing agricultural resilience in the face of climate change. Despite its impressive adaptability
column9: pearl millet yield in sub-Saharan Africa remains low. In order to enhance productivity and nutritional quality
column10: it is important to better understand the physiological and genetic mechanisms that regulate the uptake
column11: accumulation
column12: transport and utilisation of nutrients because they are critical for sustaining growth
column13: development
column14:
and resistance to biotic and abiotic stresses.

In this project
column15: we aimed at studying the diversity for leaf ion content in pearl millet and its genetic control. For this
column16: a diverse panel of 165 pearl millet inbred lines from the pearl millet association panel (PMiGAP
column18: 2015) was analyzed for leaf ion content under irrigated and vegetative drought stress conditions during the 2021 and 2022 growing seasons in the field in Senegal (Affortit et al.
column19:
in preparation).

The last ligulated leaf from the main tiller was sampled from three plants at 49 days after sowing in 2021 and 42 days after sowing in 2022. Leaves were washed in a 0.1% Triton X-100 solution
column20: rinsed with deionized water
column21: and stored in paper bags before drying at 60°C in an oven for three days. Leaf disks were sampled from dry leaves harvested from the field at around 5 cm from the ligule. Three leaf disks (5 cm diameter) from the three plants harvested in the same plot were pooled. Ion content was also studied in soils sampled at different locations and depths in the field at the beginning of the experiment. Plants remaining in the field were subjected to agro-morphological measurements (Day to flowering in particular; Affortit et al.
column22:
in preparation).

Ion content in leaves and soils were measured at the ionomic platform of the University of Nottingham using Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometers (ICP-MS) following a similar procedure to Danku et al (2013). Samples were analyzed for the content of a number of ions including As
column23: Cr
column29: Se
column41: Mo and Cd. For ion content in leaves
column42: Best Linear Unbiased Estimators (BLUEs) were calculated for each ion using StatgenSTA package considering a resolvable incomplete block design for the analysis (Rossum
column43:
2023).

Inbred lines were genotyped using tGBS Genotyping by Sequencing technology conducted with the restriction enzyme Bsp1286I (Freedom Markers
column44: USA). Samples were sequenced using an Illumina HiSeq X instrument
column45: and reads were aligned to the Cenchrus Americanus ASM217483v2 (Varshney et al.
column46: 2017) reference genome after debarcoding and trimming of reads. Lines with high missing data were removed and SNPs were filtered based on missing percentage (< 50%) and minor allele frequency (MAF > 5%). A total of 269
column47:
248 SNP were used in GWAS. The missing data were imputed based on a matrix factorization approach using the ""impute"" function of the LEA package (Frichot and François 2015).

GWAS was performed on BLUEs
column48: Box-Cox transformed BLUEs to achieve normality and residues from the linear regression between individual ion concentrations and flowering time. Residuals of BLUEs were calculated to correct for potential confounding effect of phenology on ion content
column49: as correlation between flowering time and ion content was observed. GWAS was performed using four mixed models: Latent Factor Mixed Models (LFMM
column50: Frichot et al.
column55: Bayesian information and Linkage-disequilibrium Iteratively Nested Keyway (BLINK
column60: 2010). GWAS analyses were initially conducted separately for each year using BLUEs
column61: and p-values for each method were then combined across two years independently using the Fisher's method (Cubry et al.
column62: 2020). False Discovery Rate (FDR) estimation was performed for each trait to correct for multiple testing. To calculate a p-values threshold based on the number of independent SNPs
column63:
a pruning process was implemented with Plink v1.9 to exclude highly correlated SNPs. These thresholds were used to select significant SNP-trait associations. Only associations identified by at least two methods in each individual GWAS and further validated by the Fisher's combined method were considered for further analyses.

In total
column64:
161 significant associations were identified and delimited into 73 QTL regions associated with beneficial ions such as magnesium or potassium and toxic heavy metals such as cadmium.

The findings offer novel QTLs associated with grain ion content and candidate genes that are potentially valuable for breeding programs aiming at improving pearl millet leaf ion content and ultimately grain biofortification.

column3:
Tam Ngoc-Minh‡"Lien vers les données

Content of the BPI

The Bulletin pluviométrique is an annual publication
column4: first directed by Georges Le Cadet from 1911 to 1924 and by Étienne Bruzon from 1925 to 1931. Over these 20 years of consecutive publication
column5:
this bulletin provided comprehensive records of daily and monthly data for the pluviometric station network from 1906 to 1929 (24 years).

With the call for monitoring precipitation parameters for agricultural purposes
column6: the network gradually grew in size according to the Bulletin pluviométrique (increasing from 66 stations in 1906 to 316 stations in 1929). However
column7: this did not translate to an equivalent increase in data density; within the publication
column8: there are certain monthly gaps
column9: especially in the early years. In the first publication
column10:
the author notably remarked on errors related to mismeasurement due to unit conversion and/or human error.

G. Le Cadet also focused significantly on monitoring tropical cyclones and depressions in Indochina. Initially
column11: the effort was made only to list the main position and formation date of these phenomena. Starting in 1913
column12: in collaboration with other central observatories
column13: particularly Zi-Ka-Wei
column14: trajectory maps began to be estimated
column15:
along with detailed descriptions of their characteristics during the typhoon season.

No further information has been found to explain the cessation of this publication; however
column16: similar records for precipitation can be found in the BEI or BMO for the following period. These last journals only offer monthly data for rainfalls
column17:
resulting in a continued lack of daily format data.

Content of the dataset

The present dataset contains all the data from the Bulletin pluviométrique that we recover in Météo France center of Trappes
column18:
this specimen is a complete collection of BMO.

Meteorological data coverage

1906-1929

column3:
Thomas‡"Lien vers les données

The development of oasis agriculture is a turning point for humans in North Africa and West Asia. The date palm (Phoenix dactylifera L.)
column4: is one of the most critical species in these agroecosystems. It was domesticated in the Gulf region over 6
column5: 000 years ago and later spread to other regions. In the Sahara
column6:
the origins of the germplasm and the diffusion history in terms of dating and routes remain largely unknown.

To explore these questions
column7: we employed traditional and geometric morphometric methods to analyze <b>312 well-preserved Phoenix seeds from three Libyan archaeological sites
column8:
dating from the first millennium BCE to the modern period. We compared them with a large modern reference collection and Egyptian archaeological seeds from a previous study.

This study highlights the potential of seed morphometric analyses in the study of crop domestication and diffusion.

We provide here the traditional and geometric morphometric measurements that support the findings of this study. Traditional measurements such as width
column9: length
column10: thickness and area are available for Libyan seeds and the modern reference collection. We also provide the elliptic fourier coefficients obtained from the seed outlines of the modern collection
column11:
the libyan archaeological seeds and the egyptian archaeological seeds.

In the last decade
column6: the prevalence of Trypanosoma brucei gambiense human African trypanosomiasis (HAT) has fallen and HAT has been targeted for elimination.  At low disease prevalence
column7: integration of case finding into routine activities of peripheral health centres becomes crucial. However
column8: HAT case detection by the peripheral health system with limited resources requires adapted diagnostic tests and test algorithms.   The objective of the DiTECT-HAT-WP2 study was to determine the diagnostic performance and cost of rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) performed on clinical suspects in peripheral health centres
column9: whether or not followed by serological and/or molecular tests on filter paper done at regional reference centres. DiTECT-HAT has been registered in clinicaltrials.gov under ID NCT03356665
column10: and is part of the DiTECT-HAT project
column11:
funded by EDCTP2-Horizon 2020- European Union.

The DiTECT-HAT-WP2 study was conducted in centres for diagnosis and treatment and in sites for serological screening in Guinea
column12: Côte d'Ivoire and DR Congo.  In these centres and sites
column13: clinical suspects were tested with several commercially available RDTs for HAT.  Clinical suspects with at least 1 RDT positive result
column14: 1° underwent parasitological examination and 2° blood collection on filter paper for reference analysis in trypanolysis
column15: LAMP
column16: ELISA and real-time PCR in the regional reference laboratory.  If the reference laboratory tests and parasitological examinations were all negative
column17: the suspect was considered free of HAT. If at least 1 reference test was positive
column18: parasitological examinations were repeated at least twice at three months interval
column19:
unless trypanosomes were detected.

The deposited file contains the DiTECT-HAT-WP2 study protocol in French and English
column20: the informed consent form
column21: and metadata of DiTECT-HAT-WP2 in Côte d'Ivoire. DiTECT-HAT-WP2 data will be made available to qualified researchers upon request after signing a confidentiality agreement. Data requests may be sent to the coordinating investigator (for coordinates see study protocol

column3: Lucie‡Piton

These data were collected in the frame of Violaine Piton's PhD at LEGOS (Toulouse
column5: France)
column6: whose subject is to study the dynamics and sediment transport along the estuary-coastal area continuum. The evolution of the Red River delta in terms of siltation
column7: erosion and accretion of its estuaries and coastlines constitute a major concern
column8: as the delta is the most populated region of Vietnam
column9: and is the second-most important rice-producing region of the country. The Red River was ranked as the ninth river in the world in terms of sediment discharge but the construction of two dams across the river in the 1980s caused a reduction of sediment discharge from 145-160 Mt y-1 in the 1970s (Milliman and Meade
column10: 1983) to 40 Mt y-1 nowadays (Le et al.
column11: 2007; Vinh et al. 2014; Wei et al.
column12:
2019).

In order to explore the seasonal and tidal changes in flow and suspended matter dynamics in the tropical and macrotidal Van Uc river estuary (North Vietnam) and in order to better understand the sediment delivery to the ocean and the processes behind the estuarine siltation
column13:
four in-situ measurement campaigns were carried out in 2017.

The campaigns took place during the contrasting high flow and low flow seasons
column14: for each during neap and spring tides. This dataset corresponds to the dry season
column15: spring tide. Water and suspended matter fluxes
column17: turbidity and particle size distribution (PSD) were measured for 24h at three cross sections along the estuary. These three stations (ST1
column18: ST2 and ST3) are spaced by 10km. ST1 is located near to the river mouth while ST3 is the further upstream. Each station was sampled for 24 hours with cross-river transects measurements every two hours to cover a full diurnal tidal cycle
column19:
starting at ST1 and finishing at ST3.

Each series of measurements consisted of four different steps. Firstly
column20: cross river velocity profiles were measured will a hull-mounted
column21: downward-looking
column22: 1200 kHz Acoustic Doppler Current Profiler (ADCP RDI Workhorse in bottom tracking mode). Secondly
column23: vertical profiles of conductivity and temperature were measured for each transect
column24: in the deep channel at the middle of the river
column29: now released by JFE Advantech Co.
column32: as Rinko-Profiler) at a frequency of 0.1 Hz. Thirdly and parallel to CTD measurements
column33: depth profiles of floc size distribution and concentration were measured using an in-situ laser scattering and transmissometry instrument with a 90% path reduction module (LISST-100X
column39: in order to study the relationship between turbidity and suspended particulate matter concentration
column40:
water samples (~1 L) near subsurface (~1.5 m depth) were collected every four hours with a Niskin bottle. Turbidity of these samples were measured onboard using a Hach 2100Q Turbidimeter.

The Millennium Coral Reef Mapping Project provides thematic maps of coral reefs worldwide at geomorphological scale. Maps were created by photo-interpretation of Landsat 7 and Landsat 8 satellite images.

Maps are provided as standard Shapefiles usable in GIS software. The geomorphological classification scheme is hierarchical and includes 5 levels. The GIS products include for each polygon a number of attributes. The 5 level geomorphological attributes are provided (numerical codes or text). The Level 1 corresponds to the differentiation between oceanic and continental reefs. Then from Levels 2 to 5
column4: the higher the level
column5: the more detailed the thematic classification is. Other binary attributes specify for each polygon if it belongs to terrestrial area (LAND attribute)
column6: and sedimentary or hard-bottom reef areas (REEF attribute).  Examples and more details on the attributes are provided in the references cited. The products distributed here were created by IRD
column7:
in their last version.
