column3:
Thierry‡"Lien vers les données

Populations of the most important human malaria vectors in Africa offer instances of ongoing adaptations to new ecological niches set by human actions in the context of global change
column4: with the dramatic consequence of emerging and re-emerging sustained malaria transmission in new spaces and times. Hence
column5: patent adaptations to permanent irrigated schemes
column6: urban settlements and wide insecticides use lead to the conspicuous extension of the repartition area of Anopheles species
column7: and set the scene for the emergence or re-emergence of sustained malaria transmission. Physiological mechanisms allowing malaria mosquitoes to colonize new human-made ecological niches are under scrutiny
column8: however
column9:
less attention is given to behavioral modifications triggered by -or in response to-relevant environmental cues characterizing the new ecological niches.

A simple
column10: field applicable method is presented for improving parasite infectivity by concentrating Plasmodium falciparum gametocytes. Anopheles gambiae received one of the following 5 blood treatments through DMFA: (i) whole blood (WB) samples from naturally-infected donors; (ii) donor blood whose plasma was replaced with the same volume of malaria-naive AB+ serum (1:1 control); (iii) plasma replaced with a volume of malaria-naïve AB+ serum equivalent to half (2:1)
column11: or to a quarter (4:1)
column12: of the initial plasma volume; and (v) donor blood whose plasma was fully removed (RBC). The experiment was repeated 4 times using 4 distinct wild parasite isolates. Seven days post-infection
column13:
a total of 1 095 midguts were examined for oocyst presence.

This is an R script to replicate the analyses from the article entitled ""A simple and practical method to increase the infectivity of wild isolates of Plasmodium falciparum to mosquito vectors"" by Seydou Bienvenu Ouattara

column4:
ROBIN‡"Lien vers les données
This dataset includes 1
column5: 485 raster files (.gri format) representing global maps of habitat suitability probability for the most widespread European endemic plant species. 272 species are already recorded as naturalized outside Europe and 1
column6: 213 species are not yet recorded as naturalized outside Europe but might become so in the future depending on their habitat suitability probabilities. The spatial resolution is 0.4166667° × 0.4166667°. The geographic coordinate system is World Geodetic System 1984 (EPSG: 4326).    To comprehensively describe the distribution of the species in Europe
column7: we combined occurrence records from six sources: the ‘Global Biodiversity Information Facility' (GBIF)
column8: the ‘European Vegetation Archive' (EVA)
column9: the ‘EU-Forest' dataset
column10: the ‘Atlas Florae Europaeae'
column11: the ‘Plant Functional Diversity of Grasslands' network (DIVGRASS) and the digital atlas of the German flora. When several occurrence records from these different sources were duplicated on the same cell
column12: only one occurrence record per species was kept to avoid pseudoreplication.    We defined six environmental variables to model and project species expected ranges: annual mean temperature (°C)
column13: annual precipitation (mm)
column14: precipitation seasonality (yearly coefficient of variation) representing the period 1979-2013 provided by the CHELSA climate database
column15: the percentage of each grid cell with primary land cover based on the Harmonized Global Land Use models
column16: organic carbon content (g per kg) and pH in the first 15 cm of soil from the global gridded soil information database SoilGrids. Environmental variables were aggregated (using the mean value) to the resolution of 0.42° × 0.42°.    Six species distribution modelling (SDM) methods including generalized additive models
column19: maximum entropy
column20: multivariate adaptive regression splines and random forest were used. To combine the predictive capability of the six SDMs
column21: their projections were aggregated into a consensus projection. To ensure the quality of the ensemble SDM
column22: we only kept the projections for which the accuracy estimated by AUC and TSS were higher than 0.8 and 0.6
column23: respectively
column24: and each SDM was weighted proportional to its TSS evaluation. The species distribution modelling workflow was performed within the ‘biomod2' R platform."‡IRD; Alien species; Global change; Naturalization; Species distribution model; Geography

column3: Agnieszka‡Klonowska

Agnieszka‡"Lien vers les données

In Burkina Faso
column5: studies have been initiated on the diversity of rice rhizosphere and root microbiomes using metabarcoding (Barro et al.
column6: 2022) and culturable bacteria (Sondo et al.
column7:
2023) approaches to analyse the effects of rice production systems (irrigated cropping areas vs rainfed lowland conditions) on microbiome diversity. As a continuity of these studies we aimed to develop a large collection of culturable rice-associated bacteria from different rice fields in Burkina Faso to serve as a valuable genetic resource for screening biostimulant and biocontrol strains.

In this regard
column8: root samples were collected in rice fields over three consecutive years (2017 to 2019) at 22 sites in two studied rice growing regions in western Burkina Faso (Barro et al.
column9: 2021; Barro et al.
column11: and at different rice plant development stages. Phenotypically distinct bacterial colonies obtained from whole roots (total microflora) or from surface sterilised roots (putative endophytes) and subsequently plated on five different culture media (TSA of 10 and 50%
column12: NGN
column13: FNb
column14: Baz
column15: PCAT) were reisolated for further taxonomic and phenotypic characterization. Over 3
column16: 800 rice root-associated bacteria were isolated from five bacterial culture media. A working collection of 1
column17:
013 isolates was characterized at the taxonomic (by 16S fragment sequencing) and phenotypic (in vitro PGP capacity) levels.

The results obtained for 1013 isolates are presented in the table as metadata concerning isolate identity

column3:
Lucas‡"Lien vers les données

Description of the model

The model is described in Bonnin et al
column4: Predicting the effects of climate change on dengue vectors densities in Southeast Asia through process-based modeling
column5:
submitted
The original model was developped by Tran et al 2020
Tran
column6: A. et al. Complementarity of empirical and process-based approaches to modelling mosquito population dynamics with Aedes albopictus as an example—Application to the development of an operational mapping tool of vector populations. PLOS ONE 15
column7:
e0227407 (2020).

The mosquito model was not designed to provide an absolute value of adult mosquito density (e.g. as a nb mosquitoes/Ha) but rather a relative index serving as a way to estimate spatial and temporal variations of adult mosquito density. This index of density has no unit.
Two mosquito species: Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus -> main vectors of arboviruses such as the Dengue virus (DENV).
Model outputs represent seasonal variations of adult mosquito densities of a typical year representative of a period (contemporary period
column12:
at a monthly resolution.
Model outputs are given at a spatial resolution of 1/6 degrees (~16-18km) for an area covering the following countries: Thailand
column13: Vietnam
column14: Lao PDR
column15: Myanmar
column16:
Cambodia
Model outputs representative of the contemporary period rely on gridded observation of climate variables (temperature and rainfall) from the WorldClim data repository (https://www.worldclim.org/data/cmip6/cmip6climate.html) and Species Distribution Modelling (cf Bonnin et al)
Projected model outputs rely on gridded projections of climate variables from the WorldClim data repository
column19: CanESM5
column20: IPSL-CM6A-LR
column21: MIROC-ES2L
column23: MRI-ESM2-0
column25:
GFDL-ESM4 + WCmodels_mean: an average of the outputs of all these climate models
    - 4 scenarios of greenhouse gas emissions
column26: the Shared Socio-Economic Pathways from the CMIP6: SSP1 2.6
column31:
2081-2100
The density index was designed to vary between 0 and 1 over the spatial extent of the study for the contemporary period. Density indices representative of future projections can exceed the value of 1.
Model outputs data corresponding to future projections were also presented as anomalies
column32: i.e. a ratio of projected to contemporary density indices. Values lesser than 1 correspond to a future decrease of densities. Values greater than 1 correspond to a future decrease of densities. An anomaly ratio of 2 correspond to a situation with twice the mosquito density in the future
column33:
relative to the contemporary situation.

Description of the model output data

Model outputs are available as Geotiff rasters
column34:
with 12 bands corresponding to the 12 calendar months.
Geotiff files are stored in the following folders:
    > contemporary: This folder contains the following geotiffs:
        - {species}_density_index.tif: Seasonal variations of the density index of the corresponding species (12 bands)
    > projections: This folder contains the following geotiffs
column35:
describing future anomalies of density index
        - {species}_anomaly_ratio_{scenario}_{period}_{model}.tif

column4:
Jeremy T.‡"Lien vers les données

Recent epidemiologic data suggested an increased mortality and morbidity in individuals harboring high densities of Loa loa microfilariae in the blood
column5:
underscoring the importance of studies on the possible reasons for this excess mortality.

As part of the MorLo project
column6: this <b>cross-sectional study assessed arterial stiffness and cardiovascular health markers using a point-of-care device (pOpmètre) among 991 sex-
column7:
age- and residency-matched individuals living in rural forested areas of Congo. These data were collected between May 2022 and June 2023.

Analyses included known cardiovascular risk factors (body mass index
column8: smoking
column9: blood pressure
column10: creatininemia) and parasitological covariates (onchocerciasis
column11: asymptomatic malaria
column12: soil-transmitted helminths and Loa loa microfilaremia). People with microfilaremia were more likely to have levels of cardiovascular markers indicating arterial stiffness and deteriorated cardiovascular health
column13:
compared with those without microfilaremia.

column3:
Robin‡"Lien vers les données
This dataset focuses on 28 threatened woody taxa endemic to the Mascarene Islands
column4: including 10 taxa strictly endemic to Reunion. These taxa account for 53 % of all threatened shrubs and trees endemic to the archipelago and still occurring on Reunion Island. Thirteen threatened endemic tree taxa are categorised as critically endangered (CR)
column5: nine as endangered (EN) and six as vulnerable (VU). A total of 182 subpopulations were resurveyed in 109 sites. Each site hosted from one to six threatened endemic tree taxa.  Historical records were botanical relevés dating from 1998 to 2018 with data on numbers of juvenile (in the broad sense
column6: i.e.
column7: including juveniles in the strict sense
column8: seedlings and saplings) and adult (i.e.
column9: mature) threatened endemic trees compiled in conservation master plans and national action plans. Each site was resurveyed in 2023-2024 and the current numbers of adult and juvenile threatened endemic trees were recorded in a circular area of 2
column10: 500 m² (radius of 28 m). At each site
column11: we estimated the temporal trend of adult and juvenile threatened endemic trees as the difference between the current and the historical numbers of individuals divided by the number of years since the historical census to account for their different dates. A positive value indicates an increasing subpopulation size
column12: zero indicates a stable subpopulation and a negative value indicates a decreasing subpopulation. A concentric quadrat of 100 m² was set up and the number of invasive alien plants in the upper vegetation layer
column13: i.e.
column14: with a diameter at breast height (DBH) ≥ 1 cm
column15: was counted. Five quadrats of 1 m² positioned at each corner of the 100 m² quadrat and in its centre were used to count the number of invasive alien plants with a DBH < 1 cm in the understory layer (including juveniles of woody invasive alien plants). For clonal plants
column16: we enumerated the number of ramets. The abundance of IAPs with a DBH < 1 cm in the five quadrats of 1 m² was extrapolated to 100 m². The taxonomical referential of the vascular flora of Reunion was used to check the biogeographical and invasive status of each taxon.   Three functional traits and the IUCN Red List category of extinction risk were assigned to each threatened endemic tree taxa. The three traits are those of the leaf–height–seed plant ecology strategy scheme: i) the mean SLA
column18: the amount of light-capturing surface area that is deployed with a given investment of dry mass
column19: which expresses the fundamental acquisition–conservation trade-off; ii) the maximum plant height
column20: which reflects a trade-off for biophysical constraints in determining water fluxes within the plant and which is related to its competitive ability ; and iii) the mean seed mass
column21: which reflects variation in dispersal capability and seedling survivorship. These traits were retrieved from the recently established TREFL database (Adrien et al.
column22: 2025). Seed mass was lacking for two threatened endemic tree taxa in the current version of the database. Seed mass of Dombeya blattiolens (Malvaceae) was considered the same as that of the closely-related D. ficulnea (3.2 mg). Seed mass of Pyrostria commersonii (Rubiaceae) was considered the same as that of P. bibracteata (39.2 mg)
column23: a congeneric taxon distributed across islands of the South-West Indian Ocean. SLA was missing for Hernandia mascarenensis. The annual rainfall and elevation of each site were also indicated."‡IRD; Extinction; Invasive alien plant; Population trend; Survival; Ecosystems and natural habitat; Plant sciences; INRAE‡Survey data‡"Appendix_S1_metadata.tab; Appendix_S1.tab"‡‡‡English‡55.14807129744441‡55.90063477400691‡‡‡

column3:
Elise‡"Lien vers les données

Plant traits are closely linked to species distribution patterns and community assembly processes. Trait-based approaches allows scientists to understand and predict how plant communities may respond to disturbances such as biological invasions
column4: land-use changes
column5:
and climate variability.

This dataset compiles data on leaf morphological traits (leaf area
column6: leaf fresh weight
column7: leaf dry weight
column8: SLA
column9: LDMC)
column10: leaf chemical composition (N
column11: C
column12: P
column13: K
column14: Ca
column15: Mg)
column16: life-history traits (life form and maximum height)
column18: number per fruit) and a range of other ecological (pollinators
column19: seed dispersers
column20: climatic preference) and biogeographical attributes (nativeness
column21:
endemism) for > 200 native and alien vascular plant species of Reunion Island.

Infraspecific taxa were grouped at the species level.

The database includes both primary data (leaf traits measured on > 4
column23:

Contents of the RMT

The Résumé Mensuel du Temps has been published by the Service Météorologique de l'Indochine from 1949 to 1954
column4: under the direction of the Haut Commissariat de France en Indochine
column5:
and has been transferred in 1955 with the same title to the Meteorological Direction of the Ministry of Civil engineering and Communication of the Viêt-Nam Republic (commonly known as Republic of South Viêt-Nam) and published until 1973.

During the first period (1949-51)
column6: RMT was simply an in folio page (A0) folded in 4
column7:
forming a monthly publication of 8 pages (A4) giving:

  • Page 1: a short summary of the current month weather evolution;
  • Pages 2
column8: 3 and 4: daily data of 23 stations in Indochina
column9: including 14 stations in Vietnam (from north to south)
column10: i.e.
column11: minimum and maximum of temperatures
column12:
and total rainfall of the day (mm);
  • Page 5: aeronautical climatology observations (monthly averages of atmospheric pressure
column13: visibility
column14: wind direction
column15: cloud base height) from the following aerodromes: Hai Phong
column19:
Phnom-Penh;
  • Pages 6 and 7: monthly data on temperature
column20: humidity
column21: evaporation and rainfall for 29 weather stations in Indochina
column22:
including 18 in Vietnam (from north to south);
  • The last page: reproduce every month the map of the Indochinese meteorological station network.

    • After this period
column23: the parameters increase significantly in terms of quantity with the full list detailed in “Type of data”. Next
column26: daily observations of atmospheric pressure and temperature for 6 meteorological stations were presented in the published documents (Saigon
column29: Nha Trang
column31:
Vientiane) along with quick appearance of dew temperature in 1954 and 1955.

From 1955 until 1973
column32: the RMT became a more substantial periodical published in a bilingual Vietnamese/French edition. It is still a monthly publication
column34:
with only daily rainfall data still published.

In 1956
column35: aeronautical climatology observations took a new step forward with the publication of atmospheric data with vertical profile as different pressure levels
column36:
thanks to the upper air observation of balloons with radiosondes from the Saigon meteorological station.

The number of meteorological
column38: and pluviometric stations also fluctuated significantly due to the impact of the Indochina War (against France) and the Vietnam War (against United States of America). After the end of the war against the Colonial French rulers
column39: observations from North Vietnam stations were no longer published. By 1958
column41: as did those in Laos after 1962. Subsequently
column42: RMT only published data from the stations in the “Republic of South Vietnam”
column43: with a sharp decline in the number of pluviometric stations
column44:
one of the aftermaths of the intensification of the American War.

Content of the dataset

This dataset contains all the daily meteorological data that have been published in the Résumé Mensuel du Temps en Indochine.

Meteorological data coverage

1949-1973

NB:
  • 1964: 10-day records are missing
column45:
but the monthly record is available.
  • 1973: Records for the latter half of the year are missing.
